title: Short title, extent and commencement
chapter: Chapter I – Preliminary
full_text: This Act may be called the Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898. It extends to the whole of Pakistan. It shall come into force on the first day of July, 1898.
plain_language: This section names the law, defines its geographic scope as all of Pakistan, and establishes when it came into force.
legal_scope: Covers only the title, territorial extent, and commencement of the CrPC. Does NOT define any substantive criminal procedure.
misuse_risks: Sometimes cited to argue non-applicability in certain territories; however the CrPC applies across all of Pakistan including formerly merged tribal areas subject to subsequent amendments.
use_in_ai_rag: Use for jurisdiction and applicability classification queries. Filter out when question pertains to substantive rights or procedures.

title: Definitions
chapter: Chapter I – Preliminary
full_text: In this Code, unless there is anything repugnant in the subject or context: (a) 'charge' includes any head of charge when the charge contains more than one head; (b) 'cognizable offence' means an offence for, and 'cognizable case' means a case in, which a police officer may, in accordance with the second schedule or under any other law for the time being in force, arrest without warrant; (c) 'complaint' means the allegation made orally or in writing to a Magistrate, with a view to his taking action under this Code, that some person whether known or unknown has committed an offence, but it does not include the report of a police officer; (d) 'High Court' means the High Court exercising jurisdiction in the place in question; (e) 'inquiry' means every inquiry other than a trial conducted under this Code by a Magistrate or Court; (f) 'investigation' includes all the proceedings under this Code for the collection of evidence conducted by a police officer or by any person other than a Magistrate who is authorised by a Magistrate in this behalf; (g) 'judicial proceeding' includes any proceeding in the course of which evidence is or may be legally taken on oath; (h) 'local area' means an area administered by a local authority; (i) 'local authority' includes a municipal committee, district board or body of commissioners by whatever name called entrusted by the Government with the management and administration of a local fund; (j) 'non-cognizable offence' means an offence for, and 'non-cognizable case' means a case in, which a police officer may not arrest without warrant; (k) 'offence' means any act or omission made punishable by any law for the time being in force and includes any act in respect of which a complaint may be made under section 20 of the Cattle-Trespass Act, 1871; (l) 'officer in charge of a police station' includes, when the officer in charge of the police station is absent from the station-house or unable from illness or other cause to perform his duties, the police officer present at the station-house who is next in rank to such officer; (m) 'place' includes a house, building, tent, vehicle and vessel; (n) 'pleader', when used with reference to any proceeding in any Court, means a person authorised by or under any law for the time being in force, to practise in such Court; (o) 'police station' means any post or place declared generally or specially by the Provincial Government to be a police station and includes any local area specified by the Provincial Government in this behalf; (p) 'Public Prosecutor' means any person appointed under section 492, and includes any person acting under the directions of a Public Prosecutor, and also includes any police officer conducting a prosecution before a Magistrate, in a case in which he is empowered by law to do so; (q) 'sub-division' means a sub-division of a district; (r) 'summons case' means a case relating to an offence and not being a warrant case; (s) 'warrant case' means a case relating to an offence punishable with death, transportation or imprisonment for a term exceeding two years.
plain_language: This section provides definitions for key legal terms used throughout the CrPC, including cognizable and non-cognizable offences, complaints, investigations, and inquiries.
legal_scope: Strictly definitional. Establishes the meaning of statutory terms for interpretation purposes only. Does NOT create rights, powers, or obligations.
misuse_risks: Confusion between 'complaint' (to Magistrate) and 'FIR' (to police). 'Investigation' under CrPC is police-led; do not confuse with court inquiry.
use_in_ai_rag: Use as foundational reference for term disambiguation. When user query contains defined terms, resolve meaning via this section before applying procedural sections.

title: Trial of offences under PPC and other laws
chapter: Chapter I – Preliminary
full_text: (1) All offences under the Pakistan Penal Code shall be investigated, inquired into, tried, and otherwise dealt with according to the provisions hereinafter contained. (2) All offences under any other law shall be investigated, inquired into, tried, and otherwise dealt with according to the same provisions, but subject to any enactment for the time being in force regulating the manner or place of investigating, inquiring into, trying or otherwise dealing with such offences.
plain_language: CrPC governs procedure for all PPC offences and for offences under other laws unless a special law provides a different procedure.
legal_scope: Establishes CrPC as the default procedural law for criminal trials. Does NOT define substantive offences or penalties.
misuse_risks: Special laws (e.g., NAB Ordinance, Anti-Terrorism Act) may override CrPC provisions. Always check for special law applicability.
use_in_ai_rag: Use to determine which procedural framework applies. Flag when special legislation may displace standard CrPC procedure.

title: Powers of Justices of Peace
chapter: Chapter II – Constitution of Criminal Courts and Offices
full_text: Every Justice of the Peace appointed under section 21 within the local limits of the area for which he is appointed shall have power to arrest or to cause to be arrested and to detain in custody any person who within such limits commits a non-bailable and cognizable offence in his presence, and to hand him over forthwith to the nearest police officer, in order that he may be taken before a Magistrate.
plain_language: A Justice of the Peace can arrest someone who commits a cognizable non-bailable offence in their presence and must hand them over to police immediately.
legal_scope: Limited to arrest power for cognizable non-bailable offences committed in presence of Justice of Peace. Does NOT include investigation or detention beyond handover.
misuse_risks: Justice of Peace powers are narrow. They cannot investigate, search, or detain beyond handover to police.
use_in_ai_rag: Use when query involves citizen arrest powers or Justice of Peace jurisdiction.

title: Arrest without warrant by police
chapter: Chapter V – Arrest of Persons
full_text: Any police officer may without an order from a Magistrate and without a warrant, arrest any person: (a) who has been concerned in any cognizable offence, or against whom a reasonable complaint has been made, or credible information has been received, or a reasonable suspicion exists, of his having been so concerned; (b) who has in his possession any implement of house-breaking without lawful excuse; (c) who has been proclaimed as an offender either under this Code or by order of the Provincial Government; (d) in whose possession anything is found which may reasonably be suspected to be stolen property and who may reasonably be suspected of having committed an offence with reference to such thing; (e) who obstructs a police officer while in the execution of his duty, or who has escaped, or attempts to escape, from lawful custody; (f) who is reasonably suspected of being a deserter from any component of the armed forces of Pakistan; (g) who has been concerned in, or against whom a reasonable complaint has been made or credible information has been received or a reasonable suspicion exists of his having been concerned in, any act committed at any place out of Pakistan, which if committed in Pakistan, would have been punishable as an offence, and for which he is under any law relating to extradition liable to be apprehended and detained in Pakistan; (h) who, being a released convict, commits a breach of any rule made under sub-section (5) of section 565; (i) for whose arrest any requisition whether written or oral has been received from another police officer, provided that the requisition specifies the person to be arrested and the offence or other cause for which the arrest is to be made and it appears therefrom that the person might lawfully be arrested without a warrant by the officer who issued the requisition.
plain_language: Police can arrest without a warrant when they have reasonable grounds to believe a cognizable offence was committed. The grounds include reasonable suspicion, complaint, credible information, or possession of stolen property.
legal_scope: Covers warrantless arrest by police for cognizable offences only. Does NOT cover arrest on warrant, arrest by private persons, or post-arrest procedure.
misuse_risks: Frequently misused for arbitrary arrests. Section requires 'reasonable' grounds — mere suspicion without basis is insufficient. Does not authorize indefinite detention.
use_in_ai_rag: Use when classifying queries about legality of police arrest without warrant. Cross-reference Article 9 and 10 of Constitution for rights at arrest.

title: Arrest of persons for non-cognizable offences
chapter: Chapter V – Arrest of Persons
full_text: Any officer in charge of a police station may, without a warrant, arrest or cause to be arrested any person who, within the limits of such station, commits in his presence or in the presence of any police officer subordinate to him, a non-cognizable offence. Provided that no person shall be arrested under this section except upon a warrant or an order in writing.
plain_language: For non-cognizable offences, police generally need a warrant or written order to arrest. The exception is if the offence is committed in the direct presence of the officer in charge.
legal_scope: Governs arrest procedure for non-cognizable offences only. Does NOT cover cognizable offences or general investigation powers.
misuse_risks: Police sometimes attempt warrantless arrest for non-cognizable offences by mislabeling them as cognizable. Accurate classification of offence type is critical.
use_in_ai_rag: Use when query involves arrest legality for non-cognizable matters. Requires offence classification first.

title: Procedure when police officer deputes subordinate to arrest without warrant
chapter: Chapter V – Arrest of Persons
full_text: When any officer in charge of a police station or any police officer making an investigation requires any officer subordinate to him to arrest without a warrant (otherwise than in his presence) any person who may lawfully be arrested without a warrant, he shall deliver to the officer required to make the arrest an order in writing, specifying the person to be arrested and the offence or other cause for which the arrest is to be made.
plain_language: When a senior officer orders a subordinate to make an arrest without a warrant, a written order specifying the person and the offence must be given.
legal_scope: Procedural requirement for delegation of warrantless arrest authority. Does NOT grant independent arrest powers beyond those in section 54.
misuse_risks: Verbal orders for arrest without warrant by senior to subordinate are procedurally defective under this section.
use_in_ai_rag: Use when analyzing validity of delegated arrest orders. Check for written order requirement compliance.

title: Arrest — how made
chapter: Chapter V – Arrest of Persons
full_text: In making an arrest the police officer or other person making the same shall actually touch or confine the body of the person to be arrested, unless there be a submission to the custody by word or action. If such person forcibly resists the endeavour to arrest him, or attempts to evade the arrest, such police officer or other person may use all means necessary to effect the arrest. Nothing in this section gives a right to cause the death of a person who is not accused of an offence punishable with death or with transportation for life.
plain_language: An arrest requires physical touching or confinement, or submission to custody. Reasonable force may be used for resistance, but lethal force is prohibited unless the offence is punishable by death or life imprisonment.
legal_scope: Governs the physical act of arrest and permissible force. Does NOT cover post-arrest rights, detention, or interrogation.
misuse_risks: Police misuse to justify excessive force. The death-causing force is prohibited for non-capital offences. Arbitrary violence during arrest is illegal.
use_in_ai_rag: Use when analyzing legality of force during arrest. Cross-reference Article 9 (right to life) for constitutional dimension.

title: Right of arrested person to be informed of grounds
chapter: Chapter V – Arrest of Persons
full_text: Every police officer or other person arresting any person without warrant shall forthwith communicate to him full particulars of the offence for which he is arrested or other grounds for such arrest.
plain_language: A person being arrested must immediately be told the reasons for their arrest and the offence they are charged with.
legal_scope: Requires communication of arrest grounds. Does NOT cover right to counsel, bail, or further detention rights.
misuse_risks: Police often fail to communicate grounds, rendering the arrest procedurally defective. Failure to inform can be raised as a constitutional violation under Article 10.
use_in_ai_rag: Use when analyzing procedural validity of an arrest. Pair with Article 10 for constitutional rights dimension.

title: Time of detention in custody
chapter: Chapter V – Arrest of Persons
full_text: No police officer shall detain in custody a person arrested without warrant for a longer period than under all the circumstances of the case is reasonable, and such period shall not, in the absence of a special order of a Magistrate under section 167, exceed twenty-four hours exclusive of the time necessary for the journey from the place of arrest to the Magistrate's Court.
plain_language: Police cannot hold an arrested person for more than 24 hours without producing them before a Magistrate, unless a special extension is granted under section 167.
legal_scope: Limits police custody to 24 hours. Does NOT cover remand, bail, or post-Magistrate detention.
misuse_risks: Police frequently violate the 24-hour rule. Magistrates granting remand under section 167 must independently verify necessity — rubber-stamping remand is illegal.
use_in_ai_rag: Use for queries about maximum police detention without court order. Pair with section 167 for remand extension scenarios.

title: Information in cognizable cases (FIR)
chapter: Chapter XIV – Information to the Police and their Powers of Investigation
full_text: Every information relating to the commission of a cognizable offence if given orally to an officer in charge of a police station, shall be reduced to writing by him or under his direction, and be read over to the informant; and every such information, whether given in writing or reduced to writing as aforesaid, shall be signed by the person giving it, and the substance thereof shall be entered in a book to be kept by such officer in such form as the Provincial Government may prescribe in this behalf.
plain_language: When a cognizable offence is reported to the police station in-charge, it must be written down (FIR), read back to the informant, signed by them, and recorded in the station diary.
legal_scope: Covers only the registration procedure for FIR in cognizable cases. Does NOT deal with arrest, investigation steps, bail, or outcome.
misuse_risks: Police sometimes refuse FIR registration, which is illegal in cognizable offences. FIR is not a finding of guilt. Registering FIR does not mean arrest is mandatory.
use_in_ai_rag: Use for FIR registration, complaint intake, cognizable offence classification queries. Do NOT use for bail, arrest powers, or investigation outcome queries.

title: Information in non-cognizable cases
chapter: Chapter XIV – Information to the Police and their Powers of Investigation
full_text: When information is given to an officer in charge of a police station of the commission of a non-cognizable offence, he shall enter in a book to be kept by such officer the substance of such information and refer the informant to the Magistrate. No police officer shall investigate a non-cognizable case without the order of a Magistrate having power to try such case or send the same for trial.
plain_language: For non-cognizable offences, police record only a summary and refer the complainant to a Magistrate. Police cannot investigate non-cognizable cases without Magistrate's order.
legal_scope: Covers reporting and referral procedure for non-cognizable offences. Does NOT authorize police investigation without Magistrate order.
misuse_risks: Police sometimes conduct investigations in non-cognizable cases without Magistrate order, which is unauthorized.
use_in_ai_rag: Use to distinguish FIR (Section 154) from non-cognizable complaint procedure. Key for offence classification.

title: Police officer's power to investigate cognizable cases
chapter: Chapter XIV – Information to the Police and their Powers of Investigation
full_text: Any officer in charge of a police station may, without the order of a Magistrate, investigate any cognizable case which a Court having jurisdiction over the local area within the limits of such station would have power to inquire into or try under the provisions of Chapter XV. No proceeding of a police officer in any such case shall at any stage be called in question on the ground that the case was one which such officer was not empowered under this section to investigate.
plain_language: Police can investigate cognizable cases on their own without needing Magistrate permission. The investigation process itself cannot be challenged just because the officer lacked jurisdiction.
legal_scope: Grants autonomous investigation power for cognizable offences. Does NOT authorize arrest without compliance with section 54.
misuse_risks: Investigation power does not mean unlimited powers. Evidence collection must comply with sections 161, 162. Illegal investigation methods (torture, coercion) are not protected by this section.
use_in_ai_rag: Use when query involves police authority to investigate without court order in cognizable cases.

title: Procedure where cognizable offence suspected
chapter: Chapter XIV – Information to the Police and their Powers of Investigation
full_text: If from information received or otherwise, an officer in charge of a police station has reason to suspect the commission of an offence which he is empowered under section 156 to investigate, he shall forthwith send a report of the same to a Magistrate empowered to take cognizance of such offence upon a police report, and shall proceed in person, or shall depute one of his subordinate officers not being below such rank as the Provincial Government may, by general or special order, prescribe in this behalf, to proceed to the spot, to investigate the facts and circumstances of the case, and if necessary, to take measures for the discovery and arrest of the offender.
plain_language: When police suspect a cognizable offence, they must send a report to the Magistrate and proceed to investigate, including going to the scene and arresting the offender if needed.
legal_scope: Covers initial steps of investigation: reporting to Magistrate and proceeding to scene. Does NOT cover interrogation, arrest procedure, or trial.
misuse_risks: Failure to send timely report to Magistrate is a procedural lapse. Courts have held delays in reporting suspicious.
use_in_ai_rag: Use when analyzing whether police properly initiated investigation. Check for Magistrate notification requirement.

title: Power to hold investigation or preliminary inquiry
chapter: Chapter XIV – Information to the Police and their Powers of Investigation
full_text: Such Magistrate, on receiving such report, may direct an investigation, or, if he thinks fit, at once proceed, or depute any Magistrate subordinate to him to proceed, to hold a preliminary inquiry into, or otherwise to dispose of the case in the manner provided in this Code.
plain_language: On receiving police report, a Magistrate may direct further investigation or hold a preliminary inquiry himself.
legal_scope: Covers Magistrate's options upon receipt of police investigation report. Does NOT cover trial or evidence admissibility.
misuse_risks: Magistrates should not use this section to stall investigations without cause.
use_in_ai_rag: Use when analyzing Magistrate's supervisory role over police investigations.

title: Police officer's power to require attendance of witnesses
chapter: Chapter XIV – Information to the Police and their Powers of Investigation
full_text: Any police officer making an investigation under this Chapter may, by order in writing, require the attendance before himself of any person being within the limits of his own or any adjoining station who, from information given or otherwise, appears to be acquainted with the circumstances of the case; and such person shall attend as so required.
plain_language: Police can summon witnesses to appear before them during an investigation by written order.
legal_scope: Limited to compelling witness attendance during investigation. Does NOT authorize arrest of witnesses or compelled testimony.
misuse_risks: Witnesses are not suspects. This section cannot be used to detain or coerce witnesses. Non-appearance may be dealt with separately.
use_in_ai_rag: Use for queries about police power to summon witnesses during investigation. Distinguish from suspect-related sections.

title: Examination of witnesses by police
chapter: Chapter XIV – Information to the Police and their Powers of Investigation
full_text: Any police officer making an investigation under this Chapter, or any police officer not below such rank as the Provincial Government may, by general or special order, prescribe in this behalf, may examine orally any person supposed to be acquainted with the facts and circumstances of the case. Such person shall be bound to answer truly all questions relating to such case put to him by such officer, other than questions the answers to which would have a tendency to expose him to a criminal charge, or to a penalty or forfeiture. The police officer may reduce into writing any statement made to him in the course of an examination under this section and if he does so, he shall make a separate and true record of the statement of each such person whose statement he records.
plain_language: Police can question any person with knowledge of the case during investigation. Witnesses must answer truthfully but cannot be forced to self-incriminate. Statements may be recorded in writing.
legal_scope: Covers police examination of witnesses during investigation. Does NOT govern admissibility of statements in court (see section 162) or accused examination.
misuse_risks: Section 161 statements are NOT admissible as evidence in court to prove the case. They can only be used to contradict a witness at trial. Do not conflate with judicial confessions.
use_in_ai_rag: Use for witness examination during investigation. Critical distinction: these statements are NOT evidence; use section 162 for admissibility limits.

title: Statements to police not to be signed; use of such statements
chapter: Chapter XIV – Information to the Police and their Powers of Investigation
full_text: No statement made by any person to a police officer in the course of an investigation under this Chapter shall, if reduced to writing, be signed by the person making it, nor shall any such statement or any record thereof, whether in a police diary or otherwise, or any part of such statement or record, be used for any purpose at any inquiry or trial in respect of any offence under investigation at the time when such statement was made: Provided that when any witness is called for the prosecution or for the defence and the statements recorded under section 161 are to be used to contradict such witness in the manner provided by section 145 of the Evidence Act, they may be used only for purposes of cross-examination to contradict the witness.
plain_language: Statements given to police during investigation cannot be signed by the witness, and cannot be used as evidence in court. They can only be used to contradict a witness during cross-examination.
legal_scope: Governs inadmissibility of police statements as substantive evidence. Does NOT apply to confessions recorded by Magistrate under section 164.
misuse_risks: Prosecutors sometimes attempt to use section 161 statements as substantive evidence. This is impermissible. The bar is absolute except for contradiction purposes.
use_in_ai_rag: Critical for evidence admissibility queries. Distinguish from section 164 (Magistrate-recorded confessions which are admissible).

title: No inducement to be offered to witnesses
chapter: Chapter XIV – Information to the Police and their Powers of Investigation
full_text: No police officer or other person in authority shall offer or make, or cause to be offered or made, any such inducement, threat, or promise as is mentioned in the Evidence Act, section 24, to any person. But no police officer shall prevent by any caution or otherwise any person from making in the course of any investigation under this Chapter any statement which such person may be disposed to make of his own free will.
plain_language: Police cannot use threats, inducements, or promises to obtain statements. Statements must be voluntary. Police also cannot stop someone from voluntarily making a statement.
legal_scope: Covers prohibition on coerced statements during investigation. Does NOT cover post-arrest detention conditions or trial voluntariness standards.
misuse_risks: Violation of this section is common in practice. Coerced confessions remain inadmissible regardless. This section reinforces Article 14 (dignity) protections.
use_in_ai_rag: Use when analyzing voluntariness of police statements. Pair with Article 13 and 14 for constitutional protection against coercion.

title: Magistrate may record statements and confessions
chapter: Chapter XIV – Information to the Police and their Powers of Investigation
full_text: Any Magistrate of the first class and any Magistrate of the second class specially empowered in this behalf by the Provincial Government may, if he is not a police officer, record any statement or confession made to him in the course of an investigation under this Chapter or under any other law for the time being in force, or at any time afterwards before the commencement of the inquiry or trial. Such statement shall be recorded in such manner as is prescribed in rule 3, Order XVIII, Civil Procedure Code, 1908, and such confession shall be recorded and signed in the manner provided in section 364.
plain_language: A Magistrate (not a police officer) may record confessions or statements during investigation. These recordings have special evidentiary value unlike police-recorded statements.
legal_scope: Governs Magistrate-recorded confessions and statements. These ARE admissible as evidence unlike section 161 police statements.
misuse_risks: Magistrate must ensure confession is voluntary and the accused is not under police influence. Retraction of confession is possible but the burden shifts. Confession recorded under duress is inadmissible.
use_in_ai_rag: Use when query involves admissibility of confessions. Distinguish from section 161 (inadmissible) vs section 164 (potentially admissible if voluntary).

title: Procedure when investigation cannot be completed in twenty-four hours (Remand)
chapter: Chapter XIV – Information to the Police and their Powers of Investigation
full_text: Whenever any person is arrested and detained in custody, and it appears that the investigation cannot be completed within the period of twenty-four hours fixed by section 61, and there are grounds for believing that the accusation or information is well-founded, the officer in charge of the police station or the police officer making the investigation if he is not below the rank of sub-inspector shall forthwith transmit to the nearest Judicial Magistrate a copy of the entries in the diary hereinafter prescribed relating to the case, and shall at the same time forward the accused to such Magistrate. The Magistrate to whom an accused person is forwarded under this section may, whether he has or has not jurisdiction to try the case, from time to time authorize the detention of the accused in such custody as such Magistrate thinks fit, for a term not exceeding fifteen days in the whole. If he has no jurisdiction to try the case or send it for trial, and considers further detention unnecessary, he shall order the accused to be forwarded to a Magistrate having such jurisdiction: Provided that no Magistrate of the second class, not specially empowered in this behalf by the Provincial Government, shall authorize detention in the custody of the police. Provided further that the Magistrate may authorize the detention of the accused person, otherwise than in the custody of the police, beyond the period of fifteen days, if he is satisfied that adequate grounds exist for so doing, but no Magistrate shall authorize the detention of the accused person in custody under this section for a total period exceeding—(a) ninety days, where the investigation relates to an offence punishable with death, imprisonment for life or imprisonment for a term of not less than ten years; (b) sixty days, where the investigation relates to any other offence.
plain_language: If police cannot complete investigation within 24 hours, they must produce the accused before a Magistrate who can authorize further detention (remand). Total remand cannot exceed 90 days for serious offences or 60 days for others. After this, bail becomes a right.
legal_scope: Governs judicial authorization for detention beyond 24 hours. Sets maximum remand periods (90/60 days). After expiry, bail becomes available as of right.
misuse_risks: Magistrates must not rubber-stamp remand. After 90/60 day expiry, bail is a right — courts have held that further detention violates Article 10(2). Police custody remand requires special justification.
use_in_ai_rag: Critical for remand and bail-on-default queries. Distinguish police custody remand from judicial custody remand. Trigger bail analysis after 90/60 day expiry.

title: Report of investigation by subordinate police officer
chapter: Chapter XIV – Information to the Police and their Powers of Investigation
full_text: When any subordinate police officer has made any investigation under this Chapter, he shall report the result of such investigation to the officer in charge of the police station.
plain_language: Any police officer who investigates a case must report the investigation results to the station in-charge.
legal_scope: Internal reporting requirement within police hierarchy. Does NOT affect rights of the accused or investigation outcome.
misuse_risks: Failure to report internally does not automatically invalidate the investigation but may be relevant to chain of custody issues.
use_in_ai_rag: Use for queries about police investigation chain of command and reporting procedures.

title: Release of accused when evidence deficient
chapter: Chapter XIV – Information to the Police and their Powers of Investigation
full_text: If, upon an investigation under this Chapter, it appears to the officer in charge of the police station that there is not sufficient evidence or reasonable ground of suspicion to justify the forwarding of the accused to a Magistrate, such officer shall, if such person is in custody, release him on his executing a bond, with or without sureties, to appear, if and when so required, before a Magistrate.
plain_language: If investigation reveals insufficient evidence to forward to Magistrate, police must release the accused on bond.
legal_scope: Covers police-level release where evidence is insufficient. Does NOT prevent re-arrest if new evidence emerges. Does NOT constitute acquittal.
misuse_risks: Release under section 169 is not an acquittal. Police may re-arrest if fresh evidence emerges. Bond conditions remain in force.
use_in_ai_rag: Use for queries about police-stage release without trial. Distinguish from acquittal and discharge.

title: Cases to be sent to Magistrate when evidence is sufficient
chapter: Chapter XIV – Information to the Police and their Powers of Investigation
full_text: If, upon an investigation under this Chapter, it appears to the officer in charge of the police station that there is sufficient evidence or reasonable ground as aforesaid, such officer shall forward the accused under custody to a Magistrate empowered to take cognizance of the offence upon a police report, and to try the accused or commit him for trial, or, if the offence is bailable and the accused is able to give security, shall take security from him for his appearance before such Magistrate on a day fixed and for his attendance from day to day before such Magistrate until otherwise directed.
plain_language: If investigation shows sufficient evidence, police must forward the accused to a Magistrate. For bailable offences, accused may be released on security for appearance.
legal_scope: Covers police decision to forward accused to Magistrate after investigation. Does NOT cover Magistrate's subsequent powers.
misuse_risks: Police forwarding accused does not prejudge guilt. Forwarding is based on prima facie evidence, not proof beyond reasonable doubt.
use_in_ai_rag: Use when analyzing transition from police investigation stage to Magistrate cognizance stage.

title: Report of police officer on completion of investigation (Challan/Police Report)
chapter: Chapter XIV – Information to the Police and their Powers of Investigation
full_text: Every investigation under this Chapter shall be completed without unnecessary delay, and, as soon as it is completed, the officer in charge of the police station shall forward to a Magistrate empowered to take cognizance of the offence on a police report, a report in the form prescribed by the Provincial Government, setting forth the names of the parties, the nature of the information and the names of the persons who appear to be acquainted with the circumstances of the case, and stating whether the accused (if arrested) has been forwarded in custody or has been released on his bond, and, if the report relates to a case to which section 170 applies, the officer shall communicate, in the prescribed form, the names of the witnesses who wish to give evidence on behalf of the prosecution.
plain_language: On completing the investigation, police must send a final report (challan) to the Magistrate listing the parties, the offence nature, witnesses, and the accused's custody status.
legal_scope: Covers the final police report (challan) submission to Magistrate. This is distinct from the FIR (section 154). Does NOT address trial proceedings.
misuse_risks: Police sometimes submit incomplete challans or delay submission. Magistrate should scrutinize challan completeness. A challan without section 173 compliance is defective.
use_in_ai_rag: Use to distinguish FIR (initial report) from challan (final police report). Critical for case filing and cognizance queries.

title: Police to inquire and report on suicide, etc.
chapter: Chapter XIV – Information to the Police and their Powers of Investigation
full_text: When the officer in charge of a police station or some other police officer specially empowered by the Provincial Government in that behalf receives information that a person has committed suicide, or has been killed by another or by an animal or by machinery or by an accident, or has died under circumstances raising a reasonable suspicion that some other person has committed an offence, he shall immediately give intimation thereof to the nearest Executive Magistrate empowered to hold inquests, and, unless otherwise directed by any rule prescribed by the Provincial Government, or by any general or special order of a District Magistrate, shall proceed to the place where the body of such deceased person is, and there, in the presence of two or more respectable inhabitants of the neighbourhood, shall make an investigation and draw up a report of the apparent cause of death, describing such wounds, fractures, bruises, and other marks of injury as may be found on the body, and stating in what manner or by what weapon or instrument (if any) such marks appear to have been inflicted.
plain_language: Police must investigate and report to a Magistrate in cases of suicide, sudden death, or death in suspicious circumstances. An inquest report must be prepared.
legal_scope: Covers police inquest procedure for sudden/suspicious deaths. Does NOT cover homicide investigation procedures which follow section 156.
misuse_risks: Inquest report is not a finding of cause of death — that requires post-mortem. Police should not delay Magistrate notification.
use_in_ai_rag: Use for queries about death investigation procedure, inquest, and suspicious death reporting.

title: Magistrate's power to hold inquiry
chapter: Chapter XIV – Information to the Police and their Powers of Investigation
full_text: When any person dies while in the custody of the police, or when the case is of the nature referred to in clauses (i) and (ii) of sub-section (3) of section 174, the nearest Magistrate empowered to hold inquests shall, and in any other case mentioned in section 174, any Magistrate so empowered may hold an inquiry into the cause of death.
plain_language: A Magistrate must hold an inquiry into deaths in police custody. For other suspicious deaths, a Magistrate may hold an inquiry.
legal_scope: Mandates Magistrate inquiry specifically for deaths in police custody. Does NOT cover general investigation powers.
misuse_risks: Police should not handle death-in-custody inquiries themselves — this is mandated to be a Magistrate inquiry to ensure independence.
use_in_ai_rag: Use when query involves death in custody, Magistrate's mandatory inquiry, or inquest proceedings.

title: Cognizance of offences by Magistrate
chapter: Chapter XV – Cognizance of Offences by Magistrates
full_text: Subject to the provisions of this Chapter, any Magistrate of the first class, and any Magistrate of the second class specially empowered in this behalf, may take cognizance of any offence— (a) upon receiving a complaint of facts which constitute such offence; (b) upon a report in writing made by any police officer as hereinafter provided; (c) upon information received from any person other than a police officer, or upon his own knowledge or suspicion, that such offence has been committed.
plain_language: A Magistrate can take formal legal notice of a criminal offence based on: (a) complaint, (b) police report, or (c) own knowledge/information from others.
legal_scope: Defines three routes to Magistrate cognizance. Does NOT confer trial powers — only commences the judicial process.
misuse_risks: Taking cognizance is not a finding of guilt. It only means the Magistrate has formally noticed the offence. Cognizance on police report (challan) is most common.
use_in_ai_rag: Use as the gateway section for Magistrate judicial process queries. Distinguish between complaint-based and challan-based cognizance.

title: Examination of complainant
chapter: Chapter XVI – Commencement of Proceedings Before Magistrates
full_text: A Magistrate taking cognizance of an offence on complaint shall, at the time of taking cognizance, examine upon oath the complainant and the witnesses present, if any, and the substance of the examination shall be reduced to writing, and shall be signed by the complainant and the witnesses, and also by the Magistrate: Provided that where a complaint is made by a Court or by a public servant in the discharge of his official duties, no examination shall be required.
plain_language: When a Magistrate takes cognizance based on a complaint, the complainant and witnesses must be examined under oath and their statements recorded.
legal_scope: Covers examination procedure at the complaint-cognizance stage. Does NOT apply to police-report cognizance (section 173). Does not determine guilt.
misuse_risks: Examination under section 200 is preliminary — not a full trial. The accused is not present at this stage. It does not give the accused a right to cross-examine.
use_in_ai_rag: Use for complaint-based (private complaint) criminal proceeding queries. Distinguish from challan-based proceedings.

title: Power to stop proceedings
chapter: Chapter XX – Trials Before Magistrates
full_text: In any case pending before a Magistrate, where it appears to him at any stage of the proceedings that the case is of such a nature that it cannot be, or in the interests of justice ought not to be, further proceeded with, such Magistrate may stop the proceedings at any stage without delivering a formal judgment and without framing a charge, in cases where no charge has been framed and the accused has not been convicted.
plain_language: A Magistrate can stop a case at any stage if it appears the case should not proceed in the interests of justice, without formal judgment and before charge is framed.
legal_scope: Covers discretionary stoppage of proceedings by Magistrate. Only applicable before charge is framed. Does NOT operate as acquittal.
misuse_risks: Stopping proceedings is not acquittal. Fresh proceedings can commence if new evidence arises. Must not be used as a tool for suppression of legitimate cases.
use_in_ai_rag: Use when query involves Magistrate power to dismiss/stop a case before charge framing. Distinguish from acquittal.

title: Acquittal in sessions trial
chapter: Chapter XXII-A – Trial Before Court of Sessions
full_text: At any stage of the trial, after hearing the parties, the Court of Session, may, if it considers that the charge or charges are groundless, acquit the accused.
plain_language: A Sessions Court can acquit an accused at any stage of trial if it finds the charges are groundless after hearing both parties.
legal_scope: Covers acquittal power of Sessions Court at any trial stage. Does NOT cover Magistrate's power (see section 249) or High Court acquittals on appeal.
misuse_risks: Acquittal under 265K bars re-prosecution for the same offence (double jeopardy — Article 13). Must not be confused with mere dismissal or withdrawal.
use_in_ai_rag: Use for queries about mid-trial acquittal at sessions level. Cross-reference Article 13 for double jeopardy implications.

title: Power to postpone or adjourn proceedings
chapter: Chapter XXIV – General Provisions as to Inquiries and Trials
full_text: Every Court may, if it thinks fit, for reasons to be recorded in writing adjourn or postpone the commencement or continuation of any inquiry or trial, at any stage of the proceeding for such time as it considers reasonable and may by a warrant remand the accused if in custody: Provided that no Magistrate shall remand an accused person to custody under this section for a term exceeding fifteen days at a time.
plain_language: Courts can adjourn proceedings for reasonable causes (recorded in writing). Magistrates can remand accused persons for up to 15 days at a time during adjournments.
legal_scope: Governs adjournments and remand during inquiry or trial. Remand under this section is judicial/trial remand, distinct from investigation remand under section 167.
misuse_risks: Indefinite adjournments violate right to speedy trial. Remand orders must be reasoned — mechanical extension without review is illegal.
use_in_ai_rag: Use for trial-stage remand queries. Distinguish from pre-trial investigation remand under section 167.

title: Execution of sentences of death
chapter: Chapter XXVIII – Execution of Sentences
full_text: When any person is sentenced to death, the sentence shall be executed by hanging by the neck till the person is dead.
plain_language: Death sentences in Pakistan are executed by hanging.
legal_scope: Specifies the method of execution for death sentences. Does NOT cover stay of execution, appeal, or mercy petition.
misuse_risks: Execution cannot proceed while mercy petition is pending before President (Article 45). Stay of execution should be sought pending appeals.
use_in_ai_rag: Use strictly for death sentence execution procedure queries. Do not use for sentencing guidelines or appeal procedures.

title: Power of Provincial Government to suspend or remit sentences
chapter: Chapter XXXI – Appeals
full_text: When any person has been sentenced to punishment for an offence, the Provincial Government may, at any time, without conditions or upon any conditions which the person sentenced accepts, suspend the execution of his sentence or remit the whole or any part of the punishment to which he has been sentenced.
plain_language: The provincial government has power to suspend or remit (reduce/cancel) a sentence, with or without conditions accepted by the convict.
legal_scope: Covers executive power of sentence remission and suspension. Does NOT affect appellate court powers or presidential pardons.
misuse_risks: Remission is an executive, not judicial, act. Courts can review if remission is arbitrary or mala fide. Different from presidential pardon under Article 45.
use_in_ai_rag: Use for queries about sentence remission, executive clemency, and pardon at provincial level.

title: Person once convicted or acquitted not to be tried for same offence
chapter: Chapter XXXI – Appeals
full_text: A person who has once been tried by a Court of competent jurisdiction for an offence and convicted or acquitted of such offence shall, while such conviction or acquittal remains in force, not be liable to be tried again for the same offence, nor on the same facts for any other offence for which a different charge from the one made against him might have been made under section 236, or for which he might have been convicted under section 237.
plain_language: Once convicted or acquitted for an offence by a competent court, a person cannot be tried again for the same offence or for other offences arising from the same facts (double jeopardy protection).
legal_scope: Codifies double jeopardy protection. Applies to same offence and same-fact-based different charges. Does NOT prevent retrial after acquittal is set aside on appeal.
misuse_risks: Does not apply if the prior trial was by a court without jurisdiction, or if the conviction/acquittal was set aside on appeal. Must have been a 'competent jurisdiction' trial.
use_in_ai_rag: Use for double jeopardy queries. Cross-reference Article 13 for constitutional protection.

title: Appeal against acquittal
chapter: Chapter XXXI – Appeals
full_text: Subject to the provisions of sub-section (4), the Provincial Government may, in any case, direct the Public Prosecutor to present an appeal to the High Court from an original or appellate order of acquittal passed by any Court other than a High Court.
plain_language: The provincial government, through the Public Prosecutor, can appeal an acquittal to the High Court.
legal_scope: Covers state's right to appeal acquittals to High Court. Does NOT give the complainant (private party) direct right to appeal acquittal.
misuse_risks: Private complainants have limited appeal rights against acquittal — state must direct the appeal. High Court has wide powers to convert acquittal to conviction on such appeal.
use_in_ai_rag: Use for queries about appeal rights against acquittal. Distinguish state appeal from accused's appeal against conviction.

title: High Court's powers of revision
chapter: Chapter XXXII – Reference and Revision
full_text: The High Court or any Sessions Judge may call for and examine the record of any proceeding before any inferior Criminal Court situate within the local limits of its or his jurisdiction for the purpose of satisfying itself or himself as to the correctness, legality, or propriety of any finding, sentence, or order, recorded or passed, and as to the regularity of any proceedings of such inferior Court.
plain_language: The High Court or Sessions Judge can review records of lower courts to check if findings, sentences, or orders are correct, legal, and proper.
legal_scope: Covers supervisory revision jurisdiction over lower court proceedings. Does NOT create new trial rights or direct acquittal/conviction automatically.
misuse_risks: Revision is not an appeal. It is a supervisory check on legality and propriety, not a rehearing of facts. Scope is narrower than appeal.
use_in_ai_rag: Use for revision jurisdiction queries. Distinguish revision (supervisory) from appeal (rehearing on merits).

title: High Court's powers of revision regarding bail
chapter: Chapter XXXII – Reference and Revision
full_text: In the case of any proceeding the record of which has been called for by itself or which otherwise comes to its knowledge, the High Court may, in its discretion, exercise any of the powers conferred on a Court of Appeal by sections 423, 426, 427, and 428, or on a Court by section 338, and may enhance the sentence, and when the Judges composing the Court of revision are equally divided in opinion, the case shall be disposed of in the manner prescribed by section 429. Nothing in this section shall be deemed to authorize a High Court to convert a finding of acquittal into one of conviction.
plain_language: The High Court in revision can enhance sentences, alter orders, or exercise various appellate powers, but cannot directly convert an acquittal into a conviction.
legal_scope: Defines High Court revision powers, including sentence enhancement. Expressly prohibits converting acquittal to conviction in revision.
misuse_risks: High Court cannot convert acquittal to conviction in revision — only in appeal under section 417. Courts must not exceed revision scope to effectively retry cases.
use_in_ai_rag: Use for High Court revision power queries. Emphasize limitation: no acquittal-to-conviction conversion in revision.

title: Bail in non-bailable cases
chapter: Chapter XXXIX – Bail and Bail Bonds
full_text: When any person accused of any non-bailable offence is arrested or detained without warrant by an officer in charge of a police station, or appears or is brought before a Court, he may be released on bail, but he shall not be so released if there appear reasonable grounds for believing that he has been guilty of an offence punishable with death or imprisonment for life: Provided that the Court may direct that any person under the age of sixteen years or any woman or any sick or infirm person accused of such an offence be released on bail. A Court may cause any person who has been released under this section to be arrested and may commit him to custody.
plain_language: In non-bailable offence cases, bail may be granted unless there are reasonable grounds to believe the accused is guilty of an offence carrying death or life imprisonment. Special concessions exist for minors, women, and sick persons.
legal_scope: Covers bail in non-bailable offences. Grants discretion to courts. Does NOT apply to bailable offences (section 496). Does NOT bar bail in capital cases absolutely — only sets a higher threshold.
misuse_risks: Bail is discretionary in non-bailable cases, not absolute. Mere accusation of capital offence does not automatically bar bail — reasonable grounds of guilt are required. Courts must not mechanically deny bail without applying the reasonable grounds test.
use_in_ai_rag: Use for bail applications in non-bailable offence cases. Apply the reasonable grounds test. Trigger special concession analysis for minors/women/sick persons.

title: Power of High Court to grant bail
chapter: Chapter XXXIX – Bail and Bail Bonds
full_text: A High Court or Court of Session may, in any case whether there is an appeal on conviction or not, direct that any person be admitted to bail, or that the bail required by a police officer or Magistrate be reduced.
plain_language: The High Court or Sessions Court has broad powers to grant bail or reduce bail requirements in any case, regardless of whether an appeal is pending.
legal_scope: Grants superior court bail powers. Both High Court and Sessions Court can grant bail. Covers pre-trial and post-conviction bail. Does NOT override constitutional limitations.
misuse_risks: High Court bail power under section 498 is separate from constitutional jurisdiction under Article 199. Courts should not impose excessive conditions that effectively amount to bail denial.
use_in_ai_rag: Use for superior court bail grant/reduction queries. Distinguish from Magistrate bail (section 497) and constitutional bail (Article 199).

title: Bail in bailable offences
chapter: Chapter XXXIX – Bail and Bail Bonds
full_text: When any person other than a person accused of a non-bailable offence is arrested or detained without warrant by an officer in charge of a police station, or appears or is brought before a Court, and is prepared at any time while in the custody of such officer or at any stage of the proceeding before such Court to give bail, such person shall be released on bail: Provided that such officer or Court, if he or it thinks fit, may, instead of taking bail from such person, discharge him on his own recognizance.
plain_language: For bailable offences, a person who is ready to provide bail must be released on bail — it is a right, not discretion. The authority may also release on personal recognizance.
legal_scope: Establishes bail as a RIGHT in bailable offences. Court/police have no discretion to refuse bail in bailable matters when the accused offers to pay bail.
misuse_risks: Police and courts sometimes wrongly refuse bail in bailable offences. Section 496 makes bail a right — refusal is illegal. The only option is personal recognizance as an alternative, not denial.
use_in_ai_rag: Use for bailable offence bail queries. Emphasize: bail is a RIGHT, not discretionary. Contrast with section 497 for non-bailable offences.

title: Bond of accused and sureties
chapter: Chapter XXXIX – Bail and Bail Bonds
full_text: Before any person is released on bail or released on his own recognizance, a bond for such sum of money as the police officer or Court, as the case may be, thinks sufficient shall be executed by such person, and, when he is released on bail, by one or more sufficient sureties conditioned that such person shall attend at the time and place mentioned in the bond, and shall continue so to attend until otherwise directed by the police officer or Court, as the case may be.
plain_language: Bail requires execution of a bond. When released on bail, sureties must also execute bonds guaranteeing the accused's attendance at required times.
legal_scope: Covers the mechanics of bail bond execution. Does NOT govern bail eligibility (sections 496/497) or bail amount determination.
misuse_risks: Excessive bail amounts effectively amount to bail denial. Courts should set bail proportionate to the accused's means and the offence's gravity.
use_in_ai_rag: Use for procedural bail bond queries. Distinguish from substantive bail eligibility analysis.

title: Saving of inherent powers of High Court
chapter: Chapter XL – General Provisions
full_text: Nothing in this Code shall be deemed to limit or affect the inherent powers of the High Court to make such orders as may be necessary to give effect to any order under this Code, or to prevent abuse of the process of any Court or otherwise to secure the ends of justice.
plain_language: The High Court retains inherent powers not explicitly listed in the CrPC to prevent misuse of court process and to secure justice.
legal_scope: Preserves High Court's inherent jurisdiction. Used to quash FIRs, stay proceedings, and prevent abuse of process. Does NOT create new substantive rights.
misuse_risks: Section 561A is not an appeal provision. It is extraordinary jurisdiction to be used sparingly. Cannot be used to re-examine factual findings already decided by competent courts.
use_in_ai_rag: Use for FIR quashing applications, abuse of process claims, and extraordinary High Court intervention queries. Always verify no adequate alternative remedy exists.

title: Discharge of accused
chapter: Chapter XX – Trials Before Magistrates
full_text: If, upon taking all the evidence referred to in section 241, the Magistrate considers, for reasons to be recorded, that no case against the accused has been made out which, if unrebutted, would warrant his conviction, the Magistrate shall discharge the accused.
plain_language: If evidence recorded at trial is insufficient to warrant conviction even without rebuttal, the Magistrate must discharge the accused and record reasons.
legal_scope: Covers discharge at Magistrate trial stage after prosecution evidence. Distinct from dismissal before evidence (section 203) and acquittal after full trial.
misuse_risks: Discharge is not acquittal for double jeopardy purposes. Discharged accused can be retried. Must not be confused with acquittal.
use_in_ai_rag: Use for discharge queries at Magistrate level. Distinguish from acquittal (which bars retrial) and dismissal.

title: Charge to be framed in sessions trials
chapter: Chapter XXII-A – Trial Before Court of Sessions
full_text: When in a trial before a Court of Session the accused appears or is brought before the Court, the prosecutor shall open the case by describing the charge brought against the accused and stating by what evidence he proposes to prove the guilt of the accused. The Court shall then consider the record of the case and documents submitted therewith and hear the accused, and take all such evidence as may be produced in support of the prosecution. The Court shall also hear the accused, and take all such evidence as he produces in his defence. The Court may, in its discretion, permit the prosecution to examine any witness after the examination of witnesses for the defence.
plain_language: In Sessions Court trials, the prosecutor outlines the charges and evidence, the court reviews records and hears both sides before framing a formal charge.
legal_scope: Covers charge framing procedure in Sessions Court. Distinct from Magistrate charge framing (sections 240-242).
misuse_risks: Charge framing is not conviction. It is a prima facie determination. Accused should not be prevented from showing documents relevant to this determination.
use_in_ai_rag: Use for Sessions Court charge framing procedure queries. Distinguish from Magistrate-level charge framing.

title: Evidence in cases where accused is not a witness
chapter: Chapter XXIV – General Provisions
full_text: Any Court may, with the consent of the accused, examine him on oath, and his evidence shall be taken down and shall form part of the record. The accused shall not render himself liable to punishment by refusing to answer such questions, or by giving false answers to them.
plain_language: A court may, with the accused's consent, examine them as a witness. The accused cannot be punished for refusing to answer or for false answers in this context.
legal_scope: Covers voluntary examination of accused on oath with consent. Does NOT compel the accused to testify. Distinct from compelled statements and confessions.
misuse_risks: This is entirely consent-based. Any attempt to coerce the accused into testifying against their will violates Article 13 (self-incrimination privilege).
use_in_ai_rag: Use for queries about accused's right to testify or right not to testify. Emphasize voluntary and consent-based nature.

title: Benefit of time spent in custody
chapter: Chapter XXVII – The Judgment
full_text: Where a sentence of imprisonment is passed upon a conviction, the period of detention, if any, undergone by the convicted person during investigation, inquiry or trial of the same case and before the date of such conviction shall be set off against the term of imprisonment imposed and the liability of the convicted person to undergo imprisonment on such conviction shall be restricted to the remainder, if any, of the term of imprisonment imposed.
plain_language: The time spent in custody before conviction (during investigation and trial) must be counted against the sentence imposed, reducing the remaining term to be served.
legal_scope: Mandates credit of pre-conviction detention against sentence. Applies to imprisonment sentences. Does NOT apply to fines or non-custodial sentences.
misuse_risks: Courts sometimes fail to give custody credit. Convicts are entitled to this as a right — the court must apply it automatically.
use_in_ai_rag: Use for queries about sentence calculation and pre-trial detention credit. Important for determining actual remaining imprisonment.

title: Acts done by several persons in furtherance of common intention
chapter: Chapter II – General Explanations
full_text: When a criminal act is done by several persons in furtherance of the common intention of all, each of such persons is liable for that act in the same manner as if it were done by him alone.
plain_language: If multiple people act together to commit a crime with a shared common intention, each is equally liable as if they committed the act alone.
legal_scope: Creates joint criminal liability based on common intention. Does NOT require each person to have committed the specific act. Intention must exist prior to or during the act.
misuse_risks: Common intention must be proved — mere presence at scene is insufficient. Section 34 is frequently over-applied to make bystanders liable without proof of shared intention.
use_in_ai_rag: Use when analyzing liability of multiple accused in joint criminal enterprises. Always require proof of pre-existing or concurrent shared intention.

title: Act of a person of unsound mind
chapter: Chapter IV – General Exceptions
full_text: Nothing is an offence which is done by a person who, at the time of doing it, by reason of unsoundness of mind, is incapable of knowing the nature of the act, or that he is doing what is either wrong or contrary to law.
plain_language: A person is not criminally liable for an act done while suffering from mental illness so severe that they could not understand the nature or wrongfulness of the act.
legal_scope: Covers complete defense of legal insanity (McNaughten-style test). Requires incapacity to know nature of act OR wrongfulness. Does NOT apply to voluntary intoxication.
keywords: not guilty by reason of insanity
misuse_risks: Legal insanity is a high threshold — merely being mentally ill is insufficient. The accused must have been incapable of knowing the act's nature or wrongfulness at the time.
use_in_ai_rag: Use for mental health defense queries. Apply the two-pronged test: (1) unsoundness of mind, (2) incapacity at time of act to know nature or wrongfulness.

title: Things done in private defence
chapter: Chapter IV – General Exceptions
full_text: Nothing is an offence which is done in the exercise of the right of private defence.
plain_language: Acts done in genuine self-defense or defense of others are not criminal offences.
legal_scope: Establishes private defense as a complete defense. Scope of private defense is defined in sections 97-106. Does NOT excuse excessive force beyond what was necessary.
misuse_risks: Private defense does not justify excessive or retaliatory force. The right ceases when danger ends. Frequently misused to justify pre-emptive or disproportionate attacks.
use_in_ai_rag: Use as gateway section for private defense queries. Must always be read with sections 99-106 which define limitations.

title: Acts against which there is no right of private defence
chapter: Chapter IV – General Exceptions
full_text: There is no right of private defence against an act which does not reasonably cause the apprehension of death or grievous hurt, if done, or attempted to be done, by a public servant acting in good faith under colour of his office, though that act may not be strictly justifiable by law. There is no right of private defence against an act which does not reasonably cause the apprehension of death or grievous hurt, if done, or attempted to be done, by the direction of a public servant acting in good faith under colour of his office, though that direction may not be strictly justifiable by law. There is no right of private defence in cases in which there is time to have recourse to the protection of the public authorities. The right of private defence in no case extends to the inflicting of more harm than it is necessary to inflict for the purpose of defence.
plain_language: No private defense against police/public servants acting in good faith. No private defense if there is time to call for help. No right to cause more harm than necessary.
legal_scope: Sets critical limits on private defense right. Does NOT apply where public servant acts in good faith. Proportionality is mandatory.
misuse_risks: Claiming private defense against lawful police action is barred. Proportionality is strictly applied — killing for minor injury is NOT private defense.
use_in_ai_rag: Use to assess validity of private defense claims. Apply proportionality test and check for public servant exception.

title: Abetment of a thing
chapter: Chapter V – Abetment
full_text: A person abets the doing of a thing who: First — Instigates any person to do that thing; or Secondly — Engages with one or more other persons in any conspiracy for the doing of that thing, if an act or illegal omission takes place in pursuance of that conspiracy, and in order to the doing of that thing; or Thirdly — Intentionally aids, by any act or illegal omission, the doing of that thing. Explanation 1: A person who, by wilful misrepresentation, or by wilful concealment of a material fact which he is bound to disclose, voluntarily causes or procures, or attempts to cause or procure a thing to be done, is said to instigate the doing of that thing.
plain_language: Abetment means helping, instigating, conspiring for, or intentionally aiding a crime. All three forms make the abettor equally liable.
legal_scope: Defines abetment through instigation, conspiracy, and intentional aid. Does NOT require the main offence to be completed in all cases.
misuse_risks: Abetment must be intentional — innocent facilitation is not abetment. The abettor's liability depends on what the abetted act resulted in.
use_in_ai_rag: Use for queries about secondary criminal liability (abetment, instigation, conspiracy). Check which form of abetment is alleged.

title: Punishment of abetment if the act abetted is committed
chapter: Chapter V – Abetment
full_text: Whoever abets any offence shall, if the act abetted is committed in consequence of the abetment, and no express provision is made by this Code for the punishment of such abetment, be punished with the punishment provided for the offence.
plain_language: If an abetted offence is actually committed, the abettor receives the same punishment as the principal offender.
legal_scope: Sets punishment for abetment when offence is completed. Does NOT apply when special provision exists for the abetment.
misuse_risks: Abettor receives the same punishment as the principal — this is significant when the principal is liable for capital punishment.
use_in_ai_rag: Use for abettor sentencing queries when the main offence was completed.

title: Punishment of criminal conspiracy
chapter: Chapter V-A – Criminal Conspiracy
full_text: (1) Whoever is a party to a criminal conspiracy to commit an offence punishable with death, imprisonment for life or rigorous imprisonment for a term of two years or upwards, shall, where no express provision is made in this Code for the punishment of such a conspiracy, be punished in the same manner as if he had abetted such offence. (2) Whoever is a party to a criminal conspiracy other than a criminal conspiracy to commit an offence punishable as aforesaid shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term not exceeding six months, or with fine or with both.
plain_language: Conspiracy to commit serious offences (death/life/2+ years) is punished the same as abetment. Conspiracy for minor offences carries up to 6 months imprisonment.
legal_scope: Punishes criminal conspiracy. Requires agreement between two or more persons to commit an offence. Does NOT require the offence to be committed.
misuse_risks: Conspiracy requires proved agreement — mere association or knowledge is not conspiracy. Widely misused in complex criminal cases to loop in peripheral figures.
use_in_ai_rag: Use for criminal conspiracy charges. Always require proof of agreement. Check if offence was completed (affects punishment under section 109 vs 120B).

title: Giving false evidence
chapter: Chapter XI – False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
full_text: Whoever, being legally bound by an oath or by an express provision of law to state the truth, or being bound by law to make a declaration upon any subject, makes any statement which is false, and which he either knows or believes to be false or does not believe to be true, is said to give false evidence.
plain_language: Giving false evidence means making a knowingly false statement under oath or legal obligation to speak the truth.
legal_scope: Defines perjury/false evidence. Requires legal obligation to state truth AND knowledge of falsity. Does NOT cover unintentionally incorrect statements.
misuse_risks: Honest mistakes are not false evidence. Knowledge of falsity is a required mental element.
use_in_ai_rag: Use for perjury and false evidence queries. Always apply knowledge/belief test for mens rea.

title: Punishment for false evidence
chapter: Chapter XI – False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
full_text: Whoever intentionally gives false evidence in any stage of a judicial proceeding, or fabricates false evidence for the purpose of being used in any stage of a judicial proceeding, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine, and whoever intentionally gives or fabricates false evidence in any other case, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, and shall also be liable to fine.
plain_language: Perjury in judicial proceedings carries up to 7 years imprisonment. False evidence in other contexts carries up to 3 years.
legal_scope: Punishes perjury in judicial proceedings (7 years) and false evidence in other contexts (3 years). Does NOT cover false FIR reporting (different provision).
misuse_risks: Do not conflate false evidence with false FIR. Different provisions apply. 'Judicial proceeding' has a specific CrPC definition.
use_in_ai_rag: Use for sentencing queries in perjury cases. Distinguish judicial (7 years max) from non-judicial (3 years max) false evidence.

title: Public nuisance
chapter: Chapter XIV – Offences Affecting the Public Health, Safety, Convenience, Decency and Morals
full_text: A person is guilty of a public nuisance who does any act or is guilty of an illegal omission which causes any common injury, danger, or annoyance to the public or to the people in general who dwell or occupy property in the vicinity, or which must necessarily cause injury, obstruction, danger, or annoyance to persons who may have occasion to use any public right.
plain_language: Public nuisance is any act or omission that causes injury, danger, or annoyance to the public or people in a locality.
legal_scope: Defines public nuisance. Requires public/general impact, not merely private injury. Does NOT cover private nuisance (civil law matter).
misuse_risks: Private grievances are not public nuisance. Must affect the public generally or a section of public. Misused to prosecute legitimate protests.
use_in_ai_rag: Use for queries about public order offences and nuisance. Distinguish from private nuisance (civil remedy) and specific public order offences.

title: Injuring or defiling place of worship
chapter: Chapter XV – Offences Relating to Religion
full_text: Whoever destroys, damages, or defiles any place of worship, or any object held sacred by any class of persons with the intention of thereby insulting the religion of any class of persons or with the knowledge that any class of persons is likely to consider such destruction, damage, or defilement as an insult to their religion, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both.
plain_language: Intentionally damaging or defiling a place of worship or sacred object with intent to insult religion carries up to 2 years imprisonment.
legal_scope: Covers physical desecration of religious sites and sacred objects. Requires intent to insult or knowledge that insult will result. Does NOT cover verbal blasphemy (section 295-C).
misuse_risks: Requires proof of intent to insult religion — accidental damage is NOT an offence under this section.
use_in_ai_rag: Use for queries about physical religious site desecration. Distinguish from blasphemy (295-C) and verbal religious offense (295-A).

title: Use of derogatory remarks in respect of the Holy Prophet
chapter: Chapter XV – Offences Relating to Religion
full_text: Whoever by words, either spoken or written, or by visible representation, or by any imputation, innuendo, or insinuation, directly or indirectly, defiles the sacred name of the Holy Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him) shall be punished with death, or imprisonment for life, and shall also be liable to fine.
plain_language: Blasphemy against the Holy Prophet (PBUH) by any means — spoken, written, or implied — carries the death penalty or life imprisonment.
legal_scope: Specifically covers blasphemy against the Holy Prophet. Penalty is death or life imprisonment. Does NOT require proof of intent under the current legal text (though courts have debated this).
misuse_risks: Highly sensitive provision. Frequently misused for personal grievances, property disputes, and minority persecution. Requires careful scrutiny of evidence. False accusation is itself an offence. Supreme Court has emphasized strict evidentiary requirements.
use_in_ai_rag: Use with extreme caution. Flag high misuse risk. Emphasize strict evidentiary standards required by courts. Do NOT provide guidance on how to frame such charges.

title: Murder (Qatl-e-Amd)
chapter: Chapter XVI – Offences Affecting the Human Body
full_text: Except in the cases hereinafter excepted, culpable homicide is murder, if the act by which the death is caused is done with the intention of causing death, or if it is done with the intention of causing such bodily injury as the offender knows to be likely to cause the death of the person to whom the harm is caused, or if it is done with the intention of causing bodily injury to any person and the bodily injury intended to be inflicted is sufficient in the ordinary course of nature to cause death, or if the person committing the act knows that it is so imminently dangerous that it must in all probability cause death, or such bodily injury as is likely to cause death, and commits such act without any excuse for incurring the risk of causing death or such injury as aforesaid. Exception 1: Culpable homicide is not murder if the offender, whilst deprived of the power of self-control by grave and sudden provocation, causes the death of the person who gave the provocation.
plain_language: Murder is the intentional killing of another person or inflicting injuries known to be fatal. Exception: Death caused in sudden grave provocation with loss of self-control may be culpable homicide not amounting to murder.
legal_scope: Defines when culpable homicide constitutes murder. Four mental states are covered. Provocation is a partial defense (reduces to culpable homicide). Does NOT cover accidental death.
misuse_risks: Culpable homicide not amounting to murder (section 304) is distinct. The distinction rests on intention and knowledge. Provocation must be 'grave and sudden' — premeditated response to provocation is not excepted.
use_in_ai_rag: Use for murder charge classification. Apply four-part intention test. Check for provocation exception. Always distinguish from culpable homicide (section 304).

title: Punishment of murder (Qatl-e-Amd)
chapter: Chapter XVI – Offences Affecting the Human Body
full_text: Whoever commits qatl-e-amd (intentional killing) shall be punished with death as qisas, or, where the right of qisas is not exercisable, with death as ta'zir or imprisonment for life as ta'zir, in addition to paying the diyat, subject to the provisions of this Chapter.
plain_language: Murder is punishable by death (as qisas) or death/life imprisonment (as ta'zir). The victim's heirs can choose qisas (retaliation) or accept diyat (blood money) or pardon.
legal_scope: Covers punishment for murder under Pakistan's Islamized criminal law. Qisas (retaliation), ta'zir (court-imposed punishment), and diyat (blood money) are distinct concepts.
misuse_risks: Family of victim can compound the case by accepting diyat — this does NOT automatically close the case for ta'zir prosecution by the state. Compounding of murder is a complex legal process.
use_in_ai_rag: Use for murder sentencing queries. Explain qisas/ta'zir/diyat distinctions. Note that state can still prosecute even if heirs forgive.

title: Punishment for qatl shibh-i-amd (quasi-intentional killing)
chapter: Chapter XVI – Offences Affecting the Human Body
full_text: Whoever commits qatl shibh-i-amd (quasi-intentional killing) shall be liable to diyat, and may also be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to fourteen years as ta'zir: Provided that where qatl shibh-i-amd is committed by two or more persons in furtherance of common intention of all, each of them shall be liable to diyat.
plain_language: Quasi-intentional killing (using a means not normally fatal) is punishable by diyat (blood money) and up to 14 years imprisonment.
legal_scope: Covers culpable homicide not amounting to murder (quasi-intentional). Does NOT cover full murder (section 302) or accidental killing (section 318).
misuse_risks: Distinction between 302 and 304 depends on intent and instrument. Prosecution often charges 302 even when facts support only 304.
use_in_ai_rag: Use to distinguish murder (302) from culpable homicide (304). Apply intent analysis: instrument used, knowledge of lethality.

title: Qatl-i-khata (accidental killing)
chapter: Chapter XVI – Offences Affecting the Human Body
full_text: Whoever, without any intention to cause death or to cause bodily injury likely to cause death, by mistake commits qatl, is said to commit qatl-i-khata.
plain_language: Accidental killing (without intent to kill or cause serious injury) is qatl-i-khata.
legal_scope: Covers purely accidental homicide without any intent. Does NOT cover negligent killing (a higher threshold applies for punishment under section 320).
misuse_risks: Accidental killing still requires payment of diyat (section 320). 'No intention' must be genuine — reckless conduct may be treated as culpable homicide.
