use_in_ai_rag: Use for accidental death/killing queries. Distinguish from murder (302), culpable homicide (304), and negligent acts.

title: Punishment for qatl-i-khata
chapter: Chapter XVI – Offences Affecting the Human Body
full_text: Whoever commits qatl-i-khata shall be liable to diyat: Provided that if qatl-i-khata is caused due to rash or negligent act, the offender shall also be punishable with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to five years as ta'zir or with fine, or with both.
plain_language: Accidental killing requires payment of diyat. If caused by rash/negligent act, additional criminal punishment of up to 5 years may apply.
legal_scope: Sets punishment for accidental killing. Rash/negligent acts attract additional ta'zir punishment. Does NOT cover intentional killing.
misuse_risks: Traffic accident deaths often fall here. Rash driving causing death triggers the 5-year penal component in addition to diyat.
use_in_ai_rag: Use for traffic accident death and negligent/accidental killing punishment queries.

title: Attempt to commit qatl-e-amd
chapter: Chapter XVI – Offences Affecting the Human Body
full_text: Whoever attempts to commit qatl-e-amd (intentional killing) shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to ten years and shall also be liable to arsh or daman and may also be punished with whipping not exceeding thirty stripes.
plain_language: Attempted murder carries up to 10 years imprisonment, along with possible arsh (compensation for injury) or daman and whipping.
legal_scope: Covers attempted intentional killing. Requires proof that the act was intended to kill. Does NOT cover causing grievous hurt without intent to kill.
misuse_risks: Prosecution must prove intent to kill — mere serious injury without such intent is a different charge. Distinguish from grievous hurt offences.
use_in_ai_rag: Use for attempted murder charge queries. Intent to kill is the critical distinguishing element.

title: Shajjah (head and face hurt)
chapter: Chapter XVI – Offences Affecting the Human Body
full_text: Whoever causes on the head or face of any person a hurt which does not amount to itlaf-i-udw, is said to cause shajjah. The punishment for shajjah depends upon the type and degree of injury to the skull and brain.
plain_language: Shajjah covers injuries to the head and face in varying degrees of severity, each with specific punishments under the Islamized framework.
legal_scope: Covers specific categories of facial/head injury under the Islamic criminal law framework. Punishments include qisas, arsh, and ta'zir depending on injury type.
misuse_risks: Classification of injury type is medically and legally complex. Misclassification affects sentencing significantly.
use_in_ai_rag: Use when analyzing hurt offence charges involving head/face injuries. Requires medical examination for classification.

title: Assault or use of criminal force to a woman with intent to outrage her modesty
chapter: Chapter XVI – Offences Affecting the Human Body
full_text: Whoever assaults or uses criminal force to any woman, intending to outrage or knowing it to be likely that he will thereby outrage her modesty, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both.
plain_language: Assaulting or using force against a woman with intent to outrage her modesty carries up to 2 years imprisonment.
legal_scope: Covers assault/force against women for outraging modesty. Does NOT cover rape (section 376) or other sexual offences. Does NOT require completion of any sexual act.
misuse_risks: Section 354 is often charged alongside or instead of rape where evidence of full penetration is contested. Do not conflate with section 376.
use_in_ai_rag: Use for assault/molestation charges against women. Distinguish from rape (376) and sexual harassment (509). Intent to outrage modesty is the key element.

title: Punishment for kidnapping
chapter: Chapter XVI – Offences Affecting the Human Body
full_text: Whoever kidnaps any person from Pakistan or from lawful guardianship shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine.
plain_language: Kidnapping from Pakistan or from lawful guardianship carries up to 7 years imprisonment.
legal_scope: Covers kidnapping punishment. Kidnapping is defined in section 360 (from Pakistan) and 361 (from lawful guardianship). Does NOT cover abduction (section 362) which has different elements.
misuse_risks: Distinguish kidnapping (section 363) from abduction (section 362 and 364). Abduction for ransom is section 364A with much higher penalties.
use_in_ai_rag: Use for kidnapping charges. Always check: Was this kidnapping (363) or abduction (362/364/364A)? Sentencing differs significantly.

title: Kidnapping or abduction for ransom
chapter: Chapter XVI – Offences Affecting the Human Body
full_text: Whoever kidnaps or abducts any person for the purpose of holding such person for ransom, or threatens to cause death or hurt to such person, or causes death or hurt to such person, shall be punishable with death or imprisonment for life.
plain_language: Kidnapping or abducting for ransom, with threats or causing hurt, is punishable by death or life imprisonment.
legal_scope: Covers kidnapping/abduction specifically for ransom or with threats of harm. Requires ransom or harm element. This is a capital/life-sentence offence.
misuse_risks: Ransom purpose must be proved — general kidnapping without ransom demand falls under section 363 with lower penalty.
use_in_ai_rag: Use for ransom-related kidnapping/abduction. Flag as capital offence. Distinguish from general kidnapping (363).

title: Rape
chapter: Chapter XVI – Offences Affecting the Human Body
full_text: A man is said to commit rape who has sexual intercourse with a woman under circumstances falling under any of the five following descriptions: Firstly, against her will; Secondly, without her consent; Thirdly, with her consent, when her consent has been obtained by putting her in fear of death or of hurt; Fourthly, with her consent, when the man knows that he is not her husband, and that her consent is given because she believes that he is another man to whom she is or believes herself to be lawfully married; Fifthly, with or without her consent, when she is under the age of sixteen years.
plain_language: Rape is sexual intercourse with a woman against her will, without consent, or with vitiated consent (obtained by fear, deception, or when she is under 16).
legal_scope: Defines rape under five specific circumstances. Sets the legal age at 16 for consent purposes in this section. Does NOT cover male-on-male sexual assault under this section.
misuse_risks: Consent vitiated by fear or deception is rape. The age of consent here is 16 (not 18). Medical evidence, while important, is not the only evidence of rape.
use_in_ai_rag: Use for rape charge definitions and elements. Apply five-part test. Note age of consent is 16 under this section.

title: Punishment for rape
chapter: Chapter XVI – Offences Affecting the Human Body
full_text: Whoever commits rape shall be punished with death or imprisonment of either description for a term which shall not be less than ten years or more than twenty-five years and shall also be liable to fine.
plain_language: Rape is punishable by death or 10-25 years imprisonment, plus a fine.
legal_scope: Punishes rape as defined in section 375. Death or 10-25 years imprisonment. Does NOT cover sexual harassment (lower penalty under section 509).
misuse_risks: Courts apply the death penalty sparingly — 10-25 years is the standard range. Anti-Rape Act 2021 has introduced additional protections and procedures.
use_in_ai_rag: Use for rape sentencing queries. Check Anti-Rape Act 2021 for additional procedural protections for survivors.

title: Unnatural offences
chapter: Chapter XVI – Offences Affecting the Human Body
full_text: Whoever voluntarily has carnal intercourse against the order of nature with any man, woman or animal, shall be punished with imprisonment for life, or with imprisonment of either description for a term which shall not be less than two years nor more than ten years, and shall also be liable to fine.
plain_language: Sexual intercourse against the order of nature with any person or animal carries life imprisonment or 2-10 years.
legal_scope: Covers sexual acts 'against the order of nature.' Remains the primary provision for male-on-male sexual assault and bestiality in Pakistan.
misuse_risks: Broad language makes this provision susceptible to abuse against LGBTQ+ individuals. However, it is also the primary legal recourse for male rape victims.
use_in_ai_rag: Use for unnatural offences queries. Note dual application: criminal enforcement and potential misuse. Check if consensual adult acts are the issue.

title: Theft
chapter: Chapter XVII – Offences Against Property
full_text: Whoever, intending to take dishonestly any moveable property out of the possession of any person without that person's consent, moves that property in order to such taking, is said to commit theft.
plain_language: Theft is the dishonest, non-consensual taking of moveable property from someone's possession.
legal_scope: Defines theft. Requires: moveable property, out of possession, without consent, dishonest intention, actual movement. Does NOT cover immoveable property.
misuse_risks: Immoveable property disputes are not theft. Taking what one believes in good faith to be one's own is not theft (no dishonest intent). Temporary taking with intent to return may not be theft.
use_in_ai_rag: Use for theft charge classification. Apply five-element test. Distinguish from robbery (390), extortion (383), and civil property disputes.

title: Punishment for theft
chapter: Chapter XVII – Offences Against Property
full_text: Whoever commits theft shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, or with fine, or with both.
plain_language: Theft carries up to 3 years imprisonment, fine, or both.
legal_scope: Sets punishment for theft under section 378. Does NOT apply to aggravated theft (section 380/381) or theft with Hadd conditions.
misuse_risks: Aggravated theft (house-breaking theft, theft by clerk) carries higher penalties under sections 380-381.
use_in_ai_rag: Use for basic theft sentencing. Check for aggravated theft circumstances (380/381) before applying this provision.

title: Robbery
chapter: Chapter XVII – Offences Against Property
full_text: In all theft, if in order to the committing of the theft, or in committing the theft, or in carrying away or attempting to carry away property obtained by the theft, the offender, for that end, voluntarily causes or attempts to cause to any person death or hurt or wrongful restraint, or fear of instant death or of instant hurt, or of instant wrongful restraint, theft is robbery. In all extortion, if the offender, at the time of committing the extortion, is in the presence of the person put in fear, and commits the extortion by putting that person in fear of instant death, of instant hurt, or of instant wrongful restraint to that person or to some other person, and, by so putting in fear, induces the person so put in fear then and there to deliver up the thing extorted, the extortion is robbery.
plain_language: Robbery is theft accompanied by causing or threatening immediate death, hurt, or restraint. It is essentially theft plus violence/immediate threat.
legal_scope: Combines theft or extortion with immediate violence or threat. Requires contemporaneity of theft and violence. Does NOT cover extortion without immediate presence.
misuse_risks: Violence must be contemporaneous with theft. Post-theft violence to escape may not automatically convert theft to robbery — timing analysis is required.
use_in_ai_rag: Use to classify robbery vs theft vs extortion. Apply contemporaneity test for violence. Check for dacoity elements (5+ persons) under section 391.

title: Punishment for robbery
chapter: Chapter XVII – Offences Against Property
full_text: Whoever commits robbery shall be punished with rigorous imprisonment for a term which may extend to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine; and if the robbery be committed on the highway between sunset and sunrise, the imprisonment may be extended to fourteen years.
plain_language: Robbery carries up to 10 years rigorous imprisonment. Highway robbery at night carries up to 14 years.
legal_scope: Sets robbery punishment. Night highway robbery aggravated to 14 years. Does NOT cover dacoity (higher punishment under section 395).
misuse_risks: Night highway robbery must involve a public highway — not any road. Time element (sunset to sunrise) must be proved.
use_in_ai_rag: Use for robbery sentencing. Apply night/highway aggravation if applicable. Check for dacoity (391/395) for cases involving 5+ persons.

title: Punishment for dacoity
chapter: Chapter XVII – Offences Against Property
full_text: Whoever commits dacoity shall be punished with imprisonment for life, or with rigorous imprisonment for a term which may extend to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine.
plain_language: Dacoity (robbery by 5 or more persons) carries life imprisonment or up to 10 years rigorous imprisonment.
legal_scope: Punishes dacoity (defined in section 391 as robbery by 5+ persons). Life sentence or 10 years RI.
misuse_risks: All 5+ persons must be party to the dacoity — mere presence at scene is insufficient. Evidence against each individual must be assessed independently.
use_in_ai_rag: Use for dacoity sentencing. Verify 5-person threshold. Assess individual participation for each accused.

title: Criminal breach of trust
chapter: Chapter XVII – Offences Against Property
full_text: Whoever, being in any manner entrusted with property, or with any dominion over property, dishonestly misappropriates or converts to his own use that property, or dishonestly uses or disposes of that property in violation of any direction of law prescribing the mode in which such trust is to be discharged, or of any legal contract, express or implied, which he has made touching the discharge of such trust, or wilfully suffers any other person so to do, is said to commit criminal breach of trust.
plain_language: Criminal breach of trust is the dishonest misappropriation or misuse of property entrusted to someone's care.
legal_scope: Covers misuse of entrusted property. Requires: entrustment, dominion over property, dishonest misappropriation or disposal. Does NOT cover general fraud or deceit (different sections).
misuse_risks: Distinction from cheating (section 415): CBT involves entrusted property; cheating involves deception at the outset. Civil disputes about property misuse may be confused with criminal CBT.
use_in_ai_rag: Use for CBT charges. Key elements: entrustment + dishonest misappropriation. Distinguish from cheating (415/420) which involves initial deception.

title: Cheating
chapter: Chapter XVII – Offences Against Property
full_text: Whoever, by deceiving any person, fraudulently or dishonestly induces the person so deceived to deliver any property to any person, or to consent that any person shall retain any property, or intentionally induces the person so deceived to do or omit to do anything which he would not do or omit if he were not so deceived, and which act or omission causes or is likely to cause damage or harm to that person in body, mind, reputation or property, is said to cheat.
plain_language: Cheating involves deceiving someone to deliver property or act against their interest, causing harm.
legal_scope: Defines cheating through deception. Requires: deception AND either delivery of property OR act/omission causing harm. Does NOT cover breach of contract without deception.
misuse_risks: Mere breach of contract is not cheating. Criminal deception (fraudulent intent from the start) must be proved. Section 420 is the punishing section for cheating.
use_in_ai_rag: Use for cheating/fraud charge definitions. Distinguish from CBT (405) and civil contract breach. Intent to deceive must pre-exist.

title: Cheating and dishonestly inducing delivery of property
chapter: Chapter XVII – Offences Against Property
full_text: Whoever cheats and thereby dishonestly induces the person deceived to deliver any property to any person, or to make, alter or destroy the whole or any part of a valuable security, or anything which is signed or sealed and which is capable of being converted into a valuable security, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine.
plain_language: Cheating that involves fraudulently obtaining property or manipulating valuable securities carries up to 7 years imprisonment.
legal_scope: Punishes cheating involving property delivery or document manipulation. Maximum 7 years. Does NOT cover cheating without property delivery (lower punishment under section 417).
misuse_risks: Section 420 is specifically for cheating involving property delivery. Cheating without property delivery falls under section 417 (2 years max).
use_in_ai_rag: Use for cheating sentencing involving property. Distinguish: 420 (7 years, property delivery) vs 417 (2 years, no property delivery).

title: Criminal trespass
chapter: Chapter XVII – Offences Against Property
full_text: Whoever enters into or upon property in the possession of another with intent to commit an offence or to intimidate, insult, or annoy any person in possession of such property, or, having lawfully entered into or upon such property, unlawfully remains there with intent thereby to intimidate, insult, or annoy any such person, or with intent to commit an offence, is said to commit criminal trespass.
plain_language: Criminal trespass is entering someone's property with intent to commit an offence, intimidate, or annoy the possessor.
legal_scope: Covers criminal (intentional) trespass. Intent is the key element. Does NOT cover accidental entry or civil trespass.
misuse_risks: Mere entry without criminal intent is civil trespass, not criminal. Intent must be proved. Section 441 is often misused in property disputes.
use_in_ai_rag: Use for criminal trespass charges. Apply intent test. Distinguish from civil trespass (no criminal intent).

title: Dishonestly breaking open receptacle containing property
chapter: Chapter XVII – Offences Against Property
full_text: Whoever dishonestly or with intent to commit mischief breaks open or unfastens any closed receptacle which contains or which he believes to contain property shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both.
plain_language: Breaking open a container believed to hold property, with dishonest intent, carries up to 2 years imprisonment.
legal_scope: Covers dishonest breaking of receptacles. Does NOT require actual theft — intent is sufficient.
misuse_risks: The offence is complete on breaking with intent — no actual theft is required.
use_in_ai_rag: Use for breaking-into-container offences. Distinguish from house-breaking (445/446) which involves premises.

title: Using as genuine a forged document
chapter: Chapter XVIII – Offences Relating to Documents and Property Marks
full_text: Whoever fraudulently or dishonestly uses as genuine any document which he knows or has reason to believe to be a forged document, shall be punished in the same manner as if he had forged such document.
plain_language: Using a forged document knowing it to be forged carries the same punishment as creating the forgery.
legal_scope: Covers use of forged documents. Knowledge that the document is forged is required. Punishment equals that for forgery (sections 463-466).
misuse_risks: Knowledge or reason to believe forgery is required. Innocent use of unknowingly forged document is not an offence under this section.
use_in_ai_rag: Use for document fraud charges. Knowledge element is critical. Pair with relevant forgery sections for complete analysis.

title: Counterfeiting currency
chapter: Chapter XVIII – Offences Relating to Documents and Property Marks
full_text: Whoever counterfeits, or knowingly performs any part of the process of counterfeiting, any currency note or bank note shall be punished with imprisonment for life, or with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine.
plain_language: Counterfeiting currency notes carries life imprisonment or up to 10 years, plus fine.
legal_scope: Covers counterfeit currency production. Life sentence or up to 10 years. Does NOT cover using counterfeit currency (section 489B).
misuse_risks: Different sections apply to making (489A) vs using (489B) counterfeit currency. Each is a separate offence.
use_in_ai_rag: Use for currency counterfeiting charges. Distinguish making (489A) from using/possessing (489B-489D).

title: Defamation
chapter: Chapter XXI – Defamation
full_text: Whoever, by words either spoken or intended to be read, or by signs or by visible representations, makes or publishes any imputation concerning any person intending to harm, or knowing or having reason to believe that such imputation will harm, the reputation of such person, is said, except in the cases hereinafter excepted, to defame that person.
plain_language: Defamation is making or publishing a harmful imputation about a person's reputation through words, signs, or representations.
legal_scope: Defines defamation. Covers both libel (written) and slander (spoken). Requires intent to harm reputation or knowledge of likely harm. Ten exceptions exist for privileged communications.
misuse_risks: Truth is a defense under exceptions. Public interest, court proceedings, and fair comment are protected. Section 499 is frequently misused to silence media and critics.
use_in_ai_rag: Use for defamation charge queries. Apply intent test and check ten exceptions. Distinguish from criminal intimidation (503).

title: Punishment for defamation
chapter: Chapter XXI – Defamation
full_text: Whoever defames another shall be punished with simple imprisonment for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both.
plain_language: Defamation carries up to 2 years simple imprisonment, fine, or both.
legal_scope: Punishes defamation as defined in section 499. Maximum 2 years simple imprisonment.
misuse_risks: Simple (not rigorous) imprisonment. Civil defamation remedies exist separately under law of torts.
use_in_ai_rag: Use for defamation sentencing. Note: simple imprisonment only (not rigorous). Civil remedies exist independently.

title: Criminal intimidation
chapter: Chapter XXII – Criminal Intimidation, Insult, and Annoyance
full_text: Whoever threatens another with any injury to his person, reputation, or property, or to the person or reputation of any one in whom that person is interested, with intent to cause alarm to that person, or to cause that person to do any act which he is not legally bound to do, or to omit to do any act which that person is legally entitled to do, as the means of avoiding the execution of such threat, is said to commit criminal intimidation.
plain_language: Criminal intimidation is threatening someone with injury to person, property, or reputation to cause alarm or compel/deter them from doing something.
legal_scope: Covers threats to cause alarm or compel action. Includes threats against third parties related to the person. Does NOT require the threat to be carried out.
misuse_risks: Mere expression of anger is not criminal intimidation. A specific threat with intent to cause alarm must be proved.
use_in_ai_rag: Use for criminal threat/intimidation queries. Distinguish from defamation (499) and mere quarrelling (504).

title: Punishment for criminal intimidation
chapter: Chapter XXII – Criminal Intimidation, Insult, and Annoyance
full_text: Whoever commits the offence of criminal intimidation shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both; and if the threat be to cause death or grievous hurt, or to cause the destruction of any property by fire, or to cause an offence punishable with death or imprisonment for life, or with imprisonment for a term which may extend to seven years, or to impute unchastity to a woman, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, or with fine, or with both.
plain_language: Basic criminal intimidation carries up to 2 years. Threatening death, serious harm, arson, or imputing unchastity to a woman carries up to 7 years.
legal_scope: Sets punishment for criminal intimidation: basic (2 years) or aggravated (7 years for death threats/arson/imputing unchastity).
misuse_risks: Imputing unchastity to a woman is the specific aggravated ground — carries up to 7 years. This is distinct from defamation proceedings.
use_in_ai_rag: Use for criminal intimidation sentencing. Apply basic vs aggravated analysis. Note: threats to impute unchastity to women get 7 years.

title: Right of individuals to be dealt with in accordance with law
chapter: Part I – Introductory
full_text: (1) To enjoy the protection of law and to be treated in accordance with law is the inalienable right of every citizen wherever he may be, and of every other person for the time being within Pakistan. (2) In particular— (a) no action detrimental to the life, liberty, body, reputation or property of any person shall be taken except in accordance with law; (b) no person shall be prevented from or be penalised for doing that which is not prohibited by law; and (c) no person shall be compelled to do that which the law does not require him to do.
plain_language: Every person in Pakistan has the right to be treated according to law. No harm to life, liberty, or property except as law allows. You cannot be punished for doing what law does not prohibit, nor compelled to do what law does not require.
legal_scope: Fundamental rule of law guarantee. Covers all persons in Pakistan. Prohibits unlawful executive action. Does NOT create specific criminal defenses.
misuse_risks: Article 4 is often invoked broadly. Courts have held it applies to executive action only where specific rights articles do not cover the situation.
use_in_ai_rag: Use as foundational constitutional reference for unlawful state action queries. Pair with specific Articles (9, 10, 10A) for particular rights.

title: Security of person
chapter: Part II – Fundamental Rights and Principles of Policy
full_text: No person shall be deprived of life or liberty save in accordance with law.
plain_language: No one can be killed or deprived of freedom except as the law specifically permits.
legal_scope: Protects right to life and liberty. Requires lawful authority for any deprivation. Does NOT guarantee right against all suffering — only deprivation of life and liberty.
misuse_risks: Extra-judicial killings, enforced disappearances, and unlawful detention all violate Article 9. Courts have extended 'life' to include the right to a dignified existence.
use_in_ai_rag: Use for all life and liberty deprivation challenges. Critical for unlawful detention, extra-judicial killing, and disappearance cases.

title: Safeguards as to arrest and detention
chapter: Part II – Fundamental Rights and Principles of Policy
full_text: (1) No person who is arrested shall be detained in custody without being informed, as soon as may be, of the grounds for such arrest, nor shall he be denied the right to consult and be defended by a legal practitioner of his choice. (2) Every person who is arrested and detained in custody shall be produced before a Magistrate within a period of twenty-four hours of such arrest excluding the time necessary for the journey from the place of arrest to the Court of the nearest Magistrate, and no such person shall be detained in custody beyond the said period without the authority of a Magistrate. (3) Nothing in clauses (1) and (2) shall apply to any person who is arrested or detained under any law providing for preventive detention.
plain_language: Every arrested person has the right to be told why they are arrested, to access a lawyer, and to be produced before a Magistrate within 24 hours. Preventive detention laws are excepted.
legal_scope: Constitutional safeguards for arrested persons: (1) grounds of arrest + right to lawyer; (2) 24-hour Magistrate production requirement. Preventive detention (clause 3) is excepted.
misuse_risks: Police frequently violate both clauses. Denial of lawyer is unconstitutional. Preventive detention laws are frequently misused to circumvent these protections.
use_in_ai_rag: Use for arrest rights, detention procedure, and access to counsel queries. Always check if preventive detention law applies (clause 3 exception).

title: Right to fair trial
chapter: Part II – Fundamental Rights and Principles of Policy
full_text: For the determination of his civil rights and obligations or in any criminal charge against him a person shall be entitled to a fair trial and due process.
plain_language: Every person facing criminal charges or civil rights determination is entitled to a fair trial and due process.
legal_scope: Establishes right to fair trial and due process in both civil and criminal matters. Encompasses impartial tribunal, adequate notice, hearing, representation, and reasoned decision.
misuse_risks: Article 10A was added in 2010. Pre-2010 decisions on fair trial are informed by this amendment. Courts have used it to read in procedural protections not expressly stated in statutes.
use_in_ai_rag: Use as the primary constitutional fair trial reference. Invoke for any due process violation query including biased tribunals, inadequate notice, and denial of hearing.

title: Protection against double punishment and self-incrimination
chapter: Part II – Fundamental Rights and Principles of Policy
full_text: (a) No person shall be prosecuted or punished for the same offence more than once; (b) no person accused of any offence shall be compelled to be a witness against himself.
plain_language: Two protections: (a) no double jeopardy — cannot be tried or punished twice for the same offence; (b) no self-incrimination — no one can be forced to testify against themselves.
legal_scope: Dual protection: double jeopardy (13a) and right against self-incrimination (13b). Both are absolute fundamental rights subject only to constitutional limits.
misuse_risks: Double jeopardy applies after acquittal or conviction — not mere arrest. Right against self-incrimination applies to compelled testimony, not physical evidence like fingerprints.
use_in_ai_rag: Use for self-incrimination objections and double jeopardy defenses. Distinguish compelled testimony (protected) from physical evidence collection (not protected).

title: Inviolability of dignity of man, etc.
chapter: Part II – Fundamental Rights and Principles of Policy
full_text: (1) The dignity of man and, subject to law, the privacy of home, shall be inviolable. (2) No person shall be subjected to torture for the purpose of extracting evidence.
plain_language: Human dignity and home privacy are inviolable. Torture to extract evidence is absolutely prohibited.
legal_scope: Protects human dignity and home privacy. Absolutely prohibits torture for evidence extraction. Applies to police and all state actors.
misuse_risks: Torture for confession is constitutionally prohibited. Confessions obtained by torture are inadmissible. Article 14(2) creates an absolute prohibition — no emergency exception.
use_in_ai_rag: Use for torture-in-custody, forced confession, and home search queries. Prohibition on torture under Article 14(2) is absolute — flag any evidence obtained through torture as inadmissible.

title: Equality of citizens
chapter: Part II – Fundamental Rights and Principles of Policy
full_text: (1) All citizens are equal before law and are entitled to equal protection of law. (2) There shall be no discrimination on the basis of sex alone. (3) Nothing in this Article shall prevent the State from making any special provision for the protection of women and children.
plain_language: All citizens are equal before law. No sex-based discrimination. Special provisions for women and children are permitted.
legal_scope: Equality before law and equal protection. Prohibits sex-based discrimination. Permits affirmative protection measures for women and children.
misuse_risks: Equal protection does not mean identical treatment — reasonable classification is permissible. Special protective laws for women (e.g., bail concessions in CrPC 497) are protected under Article 25(3).
use_in_ai_rag: Use for equality and non-discrimination queries in criminal proceedings. Explain reasonable classification doctrine. Note: protective measures for women are constitutionally permitted.

title: Jurisdiction of High Court
chapter: Part VII – The Judicature
full_text: (1) Subject to the Constitution, a High Court may, if it is satisfied that no other adequate remedy is provided by law — (a) on the application of any aggrieved party, make an order — (i) directing a person performing, within the territorial jurisdiction of the Court, functions in connection with the affairs of the Federation, a Province or a local authority, to refrain from doing anything he is not permitted by law to do, or to do anything he is required by law to do; or (ii) declaring that any act done or proceeding taken within the territorial jurisdiction of the Court by a person performing functions in connection with the affairs of the Federation, a Province or a local authority has been done or taken without lawful authority and is of no legal effect; or (b) on the application of any person, make an order requiring a person holding office in connection with the affairs of the Federation, a Province or a local authority to show cause why he should not perform his functions in accordance with law; or (c) on application by or in relation to a person who is detained whether under any law providing for preventive detention or otherwise, make an order — (i) for securing the production of such person before the Court; (ii) directing that such person be released from such custody.
plain_language: The High Court can issue constitutional orders (writs) to compel or stop government action, declare unlawful acts void, and order release of detained persons through habeas corpus.
legal_scope: Establishes High Court constitutional/writ jurisdiction including: mandamus, certiorari, quo warranto, prohibition, and habeas corpus. Requires no adequate alternative remedy.
misuse_risks: Article 199 jurisdiction requires no adequate alternate remedy. Cannot bypass appeal/revision hierarchy without showing inadequacy of ordinary remedies.
use_in_ai_rag: Use for constitutional petition, writ jurisdiction, and habeas corpus queries. Always check: Is there an adequate alternative remedy? If yes, Article 199 may not be invoked.

title: Original jurisdiction of Supreme Court
chapter: Part VII – The Judicature
full_text: (1) It shall be the original jurisdiction of the Supreme Court to adjudicate any dispute between any two or more Governments. (2) In the exercise of the jurisdiction conferred on it by clause (1), the Supreme Court shall pronounce declaratory judgments only. (3) Without prejudice to the provisions of Article 199, the Supreme Court shall, if it considers that a question of public importance with reference to the enforcement of any of the Fundamental Rights conferred by Chapter 1 of Part II is involved, have the power to make an order of the nature mentioned in the said Article.
plain_language: The Supreme Court can hear disputes between governments and, under clause (3), can take up fundamental rights violations of public importance on its own motion (suo motu).
legal_scope: Covers Supreme Court original jurisdiction including suo motu fundamental rights enforcement. The public importance test filters individual grievances from constitutional matters.
misuse_risks: Article 184(3) suo motu jurisdiction requires public importance — individual disputes should use High Court Article 199. Extensive use of 184(3) for individual matters has been judicially critiqued.
use_in_ai_rag: Use for Supreme Court constitutional jurisdiction queries. Emphasize public importance requirement for 184(3). Distinguish from High Court's Article 199 jurisdiction.

title: Power of President to grant pardon, etc.
chapter: Part III – The Federation of Pakistan
full_text: The President shall have power to grant pardon, reprieve and respite, and to remit, suspend or commute any sentence passed by any Court or Tribunal.
plain_language: The President of Pakistan has the power to pardon, reduce, or suspend any sentence. This includes death sentences.
legal_scope: Presidential clemency power. Covers all sentences. Execution of death penalty cannot proceed while mercy petition is pending. Does NOT affect civil liability.
misuse_risks: Presidential pardon is an executive, not judicial, act. Courts cannot direct the President to grant or refuse pardon. However, courts can review if pardon was granted to protect a co-accused of the pardoner.
use_in_ai_rag: Use for mercy petition, presidential pardon, and death sentence execution queries. Execution stays pending mercy petition decision.

title: Every member of unlawful assembly guilty of offence committed in prosecution of common object
chapter: Chapter VIII – Offences Against Public Tranquility
full_text: If an offence is committed by any member of an unlawful assembly in prosecution of the common object of that assembly, or such as the members of that assembly knew to be likely to be committed in prosecution of that object, every person who, at the time of the committing of that offence, is a member of the same assembly is guilty of that offence.
plain_language: Every member of an unlawful assembly is guilty of offences committed by any member in pursuit of the assembly's common object, if such offences were likely to be committed.
legal_scope: Creates vicarious criminal liability for unlawful assembly members. Requires: membership of unlawful assembly (section 141) AND offence committed in prosecution of common object.
misuse_risks: Membership alone is insufficient — the offence must be in prosecution of common object or foreseeable. Mere presence at scene does not make one a member of unlawful assembly. Section 149 is often misused to rope in bystanders.
use_in_ai_rag: Use for unlawful assembly and riot cases. Require proof of both: membership AND common object connection of the offence. Distinguish from section 34 (common intention).

title: Punishment for rioting
chapter: Chapter VIII – Offences Against Public Tranquility
full_text: Whoever is guilty of rioting, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both.
plain_language: Rioting (violence by an unlawful assembly) carries up to 2 years imprisonment, fine, or both.
legal_scope: Punishes rioting (section 146). Maximum 2 years. Aggravated rioting with deadly weapons under section 148 carries up to 3 years.
misuse_risks: Rioting requires use of force or violence — peaceful assembly is not rioting. Distinguish from unlawful assembly (no violence required).
use_in_ai_rag: Use for rioting charge sentencing. Distinguish from unlawful assembly (143) and armed rioting (148).

title: Punishment of murder (Qatl-e-Amd) — Note: See full entry above
chapter: Chapter XVI
full_text: See section 302 full entry in this dataset.
plain_language: Murder is punishable by death (qisas) or death/life imprisonment (ta'zir) plus diyat.
legal_scope: See full entry for section 302.
misuse_risks: See full entry.
use_in_ai_rag: See full entry for section 302.

title: Forgery for purpose of cheating
chapter: Chapter XVIII – Offences Relating to Documents
full_text: Whoever commits forgery, intending that the document or electronic record forged shall be used for the purpose of cheating, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine.
plain_language: Forgery intended for use in cheating carries up to 7 years imprisonment.
legal_scope: Covers forgery with intent to use for cheating. Maximum 7 years. Does NOT cover forgery without cheating intent (lower penalty under section 465).
misuse_risks: Intent to cheat must be proved at time of forgery. Forgery discovered later without proof of original cheating intent may fall under section 465.
use_in_ai_rag: Use for forgery-cheating combination charges. Intent to cheat must be proved at the time of forgery.

title: Forgery of valuable security, will, etc.
chapter: Chapter XVIII – Offences Relating to Documents
full_text: Whoever forges a document which purports to be a valuable security, or a will, or an authority to adopt a son, or which purports to give authority to any person to make or transfer any valuable security, or to receive the principal, interest, or dividends thereon, or to receive or deliver any money, moveable property, or valuable security, or any document purporting to be an acquittance or receipt acknowledging the payment of money, or an acquittance or receipt for the delivery of any moveable property or valuable security, shall be punished with imprisonment for life, or with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine.
plain_language: Forging valuable securities, wills, or financial authority documents carries life imprisonment or up to 10 years.
legal_scope: Covers high-value document forgery (wills, securities, receipts). Life imprisonment or up to 10 years. Higher penalty than basic forgery (section 465).
misuse_risks: Document must 'purport to be' the type described. Forging ordinary documents falls under section 465 (lower penalty). Classification of document type affects sentencing significantly.
use_in_ai_rag: Use for high-value document forgery charges. Identify document type to determine correct section (465 vs 467 vs 468).

title: Causing disappearance of evidence of offence
chapter: Chapter XI – False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
full_text: Whoever, knowing or having reason to believe that an offence has been committed, causes any evidence of the commission of that offence to disappear, with the intention of screening the offender from legal punishment, or with that intention gives any information respecting the offence which he knows or believes to be false, shall, if the offence which he knows or believes to have been committed is punishable with death, be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine; and if the offence is punishable with imprisonment for life, or with imprisonment for a term which may extend to ten years, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years and shall also be liable to fine; and if the offence is punishable with imprisonment for a term not extending to ten years, shall be punished with imprisonment of a description provided for the offence, for a term which may extend to one-fourth part of the longest term of the imprisonment provided for the offence, or with fine, or with both.
plain_language: Destroying evidence or giving false information to protect an offender carries a punishment proportional to the underlying offence's severity.
legal_scope: Covers evidence destruction and false reporting to protect offenders. Punishment scales with the severity of the underlying offence.
misuse_risks: Requires knowledge that an offence was committed. Innocent disposal of items is not evidence tampering. Punishment is derivative of the main offence's punishment.
use_in_ai_rag: Use for evidence tampering and obstruction of justice queries. Determine main offence severity first, then apply proportional punishment under section 201.

title: Intentional omission to give information of offence by person bound to inform
chapter: Chapter XI – False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
full_text: Whoever, knowing or having reason to believe that an offence has been committed, intentionally omits to give any information respecting that offence which he is legally bound to give, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to six months, or with fine, or with both.
plain_language: Deliberately failing to report an offence when legally required to do so carries up to 6 months imprisonment.
legal_scope: Covers intentional failure to report by persons under legal duty to report. Requires: knowledge of offence AND legal duty to inform AND intentional omission.
misuse_risks: Only applies where legal duty to report exists. No general duty for ordinary citizens to report crimes exists — specific legal duty must be identified.
use_in_ai_rag: Use for failure-to-report criminal liability queries. Always identify the specific legal duty to report before applying this section.

title: Statements conducing to public mischief
chapter: Chapter XXII – Criminal Intimidation, Insult, and Annoyance
full_text: Whoever makes, publishes or circulates any statement, rumour or report — (a) with intent to cause, or which is likely to cause, any officer, soldier, sailor or airman in the Army, Navy or Air Force of Pakistan to mutiny or otherwise disregard or fail in his duty as such; or (b) with intent to cause, or which is likely to cause, fear or alarm to the public or to any section of the public whereby any person may be induced to commit an offence against the State or against the public tranquillity; or (c) with intent to incite, or which is likely to incite, any class or community of persons to commit any offence against any other class or community, shall be punished with imprisonment which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both.
plain_language: Making or spreading statements intended to cause military mutiny, public alarm leading to offences, or communal incitement carries up to 2 years imprisonment.
legal_scope: Covers speech/publication intended to cause mutiny, public alarm, or communal incitement. Applies to statements with likely effect, not just specific intent. Does NOT apply to good faith public criticism.
misuse_risks: Frequently misused to criminalize legitimate journalism, social media commentary, and criticism of government. Courts require clear connection between statement and likely harm.
use_in_ai_rag: Use for speech/publication offence queries involving public order and communal harmony. Apply strict harm-connection test to avoid chilling legitimate expression.

title: Promoting enmity between different groups
chapter: Chapter VIII – Offences Against Public Tranquility
full_text: Whoever by words, either spoken or written, or by signs or by visible representations or otherwise, promotes or incites or attempts to promote or incite feelings of enmity, hatred, or ill-will between different religious, racial, language, regional or other groups or castes or communities, shall be punished with imprisonment which may extend to five years, or with fine, or with both.
plain_language: Promoting hatred between religious, racial, or social groups through words or actions carries up to 5 years imprisonment.
legal_scope: Covers hate speech promoting group enmity. Applies to words, signs, and representations. Does NOT require an actual disturbance to occur.
misuse_risks: Legitimate religious discourse and academic discussion should not be conflated with promoting enmity. Courts require actual promotion of enmity, not mere disagreement.
use_in_ai_rag: Use for hate speech and communal enmity queries. Distinguish from protected religious expression and criticism.

title: Dishonestly issuing a cheque
chapter: Chapter XVIII – Offences Relating to Documents
full_text: Whoever dishonestly issues a cheque towards repayment of a loan or fulfilment of an obligation which is dishonoured on presentation, shall be punishable with imprisonment which may extend to three years, or with fine, or with both, unless he can establish, for which the burden of proof shall rest on him, that he had made arrangements with his bank to ensure that the cheque would be honoured.
plain_language: Issuing a bad cheque (that bounces) for repaying a loan or obligation is a criminal offence carrying up to 3 years, unless the issuer proves they had sufficient banking arrangements.
legal_scope: Covers dishonored cheques issued for loan repayment or obligation fulfilment. Reverse burden of proof on accused. Does NOT cover all bounced cheques — must be for repayment/obligation.
misuse_risks: Reverse burden of proof is constitutional if reasonable. Cheques given for goods/services vs loan repayment — only loan/obligation cheques fall under 489F. Section is commonly misused in commercial disputes.
use_in_ai_rag: Use for bounced/bad cheque criminal liability queries. Verify: Was cheque issued for loan/obligation? Is the reverse burden proof available to the accused?

title: Powers of Justice of Peace — recording statements and complaints
chapter: Chapter II – Constitution of Criminal Courts and Offices
full_text: A Justice of the Peace shall have powers— (i) To record statements under section 200; (ii) To issue directions to police for registration of FIR; (iii) To supervise arrests; (iv) To hold inquest proceedings; (v) To record confessions and statements under section 164.
plain_language: A Justice of the Peace can direct FIR registration, record statements, supervise arrests, and hold inquests as empowered by law.
legal_scope: Defines Justice of Peace powers including directing FIR registration. Critical for cases where police refuse to register FIR.
misuse_risks: Section 22A applications before Justice of Peace are a remedy for FIR registration refusal. The JoP cannot conduct investigation or usurp police powers.
use_in_ai_rag: Use when query involves police refusal to register FIR. Section 22A before Justice of Peace is the primary administrative remedy before High Court jurisdiction.

title: Acquittal or conviction in sessions trial
chapter: Chapter XXII-A – Trial Before Court of Sessions
full_text: If the accused is not acquitted under section 265D, the Court shall frame in writing a charge against the accused. The charge shall then be read and explained to the accused, and he shall be asked whether he pleads guilty of the offence charged or claims to be tried. If the accused pleads guilty, the Court shall record the plea and may, in its discretion, convict him thereon.
plain_language: If not acquitted at the preliminary stage, a formal charge is framed. The accused is asked to plead guilty or face trial. A guilty plea may result in conviction.
legal_scope: Covers charge framing and plea in Sessions Court. Guilty plea at this stage is discretionary for conviction — not automatic. Does NOT preclude accused from challenging sufficiency of evidence later.
misuse_risks: Guilty plea must be free and voluntary. Courts should ensure accused understands consequences before recording guilty plea.
use_in_ai_rag: Use for Sessions Court charge framing and plea proceedings. Check voluntariness of guilty plea before applying.

title: Sentence of whipping when not to be executed
chapter: Chapter XXVII – The Judgment
full_text: When a person is sentenced to whipping only, the sentence shall not be executed until the expiration of a period of twenty-four hours from the time of the passing thereof. No sentence of whipping shall be executed on a female, or on a male who in the opinion of the judge of court is more than forty-five years of age, or who has been sentenced to death, or to imprisonment for life, or to imprisonment exceeding one year, together with whipping.
plain_language: Whipping sentences cannot be executed for 24 hours after sentencing. Women, men over 45, and those with additional severe sentences are exempt from whipping.
legal_scope: Regulates whipping sentence execution. Exempts women, elderly men, and those under severe other sentences. Does NOT abolish whipping as a punishment.
misuse_risks: Note Pakistan's dualistic framework — certain hadd offences prescribed in Islamic law retain whipping provisions. Section 382 provides procedural limitations.
use_in_ai_rag: Use for whipping sentence procedure and exemption queries. Check exemption conditions before applying whipping provision.

title: Suspension of sentence pending appeal; release of appellant on bail
chapter: Chapter XXXI – Appeals
full_text: Pending any appeal by a convicted person, the Appellate Court may, for reasons to be recorded by it in writing, order that the execution of the sentence or order appealed against be suspended and, also, if he is in confinement, that he be released on bail or on his own bond.
plain_language: An appellate court can suspend a sentence and release a convicted person on bail while their appeal is pending, for reasons recorded in writing.
legal_scope: Covers suspension of conviction sentence pending appeal. The convicted appellant may be released on bail. Reasons must be recorded.
misuse_risks: Bail pending appeal for convicted persons is discretionary and more difficult than pre-conviction bail. Courts weigh flight risk, gravity of offence, and appeal merits.
use_in_ai_rag: Use for bail-pending-appeal queries for convicted persons. Distinguish from pre-trial bail (497/498). Appellate court may impose stricter conditions.

title: Power of Appellate Court to alter sentence
chapter: Chapter XXXI – Appeals
full_text: Subject to the provisions of this Chapter, the Appellate Court shall have power to reverse the finding and sentence and to order retrial, or to alter the finding, maintaining the sentence, or, with or without altering the finding, to reduce, or enhance the sentence, or with or without such reduction or enhancement, and with or without altering the finding, to alter the nature of the sentence.
plain_language: Appellate courts can reverse findings, order retrials, change verdicts, or reduce/enhance sentences. They have wide reformatory powers.
legal_scope: Defines appellate court's wide powers: reversal, retrial, finding alteration, sentence modification (increase or decrease). Applies to both conviction and sentence appeals.
misuse_risks: Enhancement of sentence on state appeal is permitted. Accused appeals may result in retrial, not guaranteed reduction. Retrial is subject to double jeopardy analysis.
use_in_ai_rag: Use when analyzing scope of appellate court powers. Note: sentence can be enhanced even on accused's appeal if court so determines.

title: Prosecution for offences against the State
chapter: Chapter XV – Cognizance of Offences
full_text: No Court shall take cognizance of any offence punishable under Chapter VI or under section 153A, or sub-section (2) of section 505 of the Pakistan Penal Code (XLV of 1860), or any abetment of, or conspiracy to commit, such offence, or any such offence as is described in section 108A of that Code, except with the previous sanction of the Federal Government or of the Provincial Government.
plain_language: Courts cannot take cognizance of offences against the State (treason, sedition, etc.) or communal incitement without prior government sanction.
legal_scope: Requires prior government sanction for cognizance of offences against the State. Sanction is a procedural prerequisite — without it, cognizance is void.
misuse_risks: Without prior sanction, entire proceedings are void. Courts have held that sanction must be obtained before cognizance — subsequent sanction does not cure the defect.
use_in_ai_rag: Use for state offence prosecution validity queries. Check prior government sanction as threshold requirement before any substantive analysis.

title: Prosecution of Judges and public servants
chapter: Chapter XV – Cognizance of Offences
full_text: When any person who is or was a Judge or Magistrate or a public servant not removable from his office save by or with the sanction of the Government is accused of any offence alleged to have been committed by him while acting or purporting to act in the discharge of his official duty, no Court shall take cognizance of such offence except with the previous sanction of the Federal Government, in the case of a person who is employed in connection with the affairs of the Federation, or of the Provincial Government, in the case of a person who is employed in connection with the affairs of a Province.
plain_language: Prosecution of judges and senior public servants for acts done in official duty requires prior government sanction.
legal_scope: Prior sanction required for prosecuting judges and non-removable public servants for official acts. Does NOT protect acts clearly outside official duty.
misuse_risks: The protection only applies to acts within the scope of official duty. Corrupt acts masquerading as official duty do not attract protection. Courts have held that bribery is not an 'official act.'
use_in_ai_rag: Use for queries about prosecuting government officials. Check: Was act within official duty? Was prior sanction obtained? If not, cognizance is void.

title: Issue of process
chapter: Chapter XVI – Commencement of Proceedings Before Magistrates
full_text: If in the opinion of a Magistrate taking cognizance of an offence there is sufficient ground for proceeding, and the case appears to be a summons case, he shall issue his summons for the attendance of the accused. If the case appears to be a warrant case, he may issue a warrant, or, if he thinks fit, a summons for the first attendance of the accused: Provided that no summons or warrant shall be issued against an accused who is already under arrest.
plain_language: Once a Magistrate decides to proceed after taking cognizance, they issue a summons (for summons cases) or summons/warrant (for warrant cases) to bring the accused before court.
legal_scope: Covers process issuance after cognizance. Summons cases get summons; warrant cases may get warrant or summons. Does NOT cover process against witnesses.
misuse_risks: No warrant/summons against an already arrested accused. Process must flow from cognizance — issuing process before formal cognizance is procedurally improper.
use_in_ai_rag: Use for process issuance procedure queries. Distinguish summons cases (less serious) from warrant cases (more serious) and their applicable processes.

title: Acquittal on basis of prosecution evidence
chapter: Chapter XX – Trials Before Magistrates
full_text: A Magistrate trying an offence may, at any stage of the proceedings, record an order of acquittal if there is no probability of the accused being convicted of the offence.
plain_language: A Magistrate can acquit the accused at any stage if there is no probability of conviction — this provides a safeguard against prolonged proceedings in weak cases.
legal_scope: Allows Magistrate mid-trial acquittal where conviction is improbable. 'No probability' is a higher threshold than 'insufficient evidence.'
misuse_risks: This is not a discharge (which can lead to retrial). Acquittal under 249A bars re-prosecution (double jeopardy). Must not be confused with discharge under 241A.
use_in_ai_rag: Use for early/mid-trial acquittal at Magistrate level. This is a true acquittal (not discharge) — Article 13 double jeopardy bar applies.

title: Acquittal before charge in sessions trial
chapter: Chapter XXII-A – Trial Before Court of Sessions
full_text: When in a trial before a Court of Session, the Judge has considered all the documents referred to in section 265C, examined the accused, if necessary, for the purpose only of enabling him to explain any circumstances appearing in the evidence against him, and where the prosecution is by way of complaint, heard the complainant, the Judge shall, if he considers that there is no sufficient ground for proceeding against the accused, record an order of acquittal.
plain_language: At the preliminary examination stage in Sessions Court, if there are no sufficient grounds to proceed, the Judge must acquit the accused before framing a charge.
legal_scope: Pre-charge acquittal in Sessions Court. If grounds insufficient at 265C stage, acquittal is mandatory. This IS a true acquittal — double jeopardy applies.
misuse_risks: Acquittal under 265D is final and bars retrial (Article 13). Do not confuse with discharge (241A) which allows retrial.
use_in_ai_rag: Use for pre-charge Sessions Court disposition queries. If acquittal — double jeopardy applies. If insufficient grounds at Magistrate level — distinguish between discharge (241A) and acquittal (249A/265D).

title: Waging, or attempting to wage war, or abetting waging of war, against Pakistan
chapter: Chapter VI – Offences Against the State
full_text: Whoever wages war against Pakistan, or attempts to wage such war, or abets the waging of such war, shall be punished with death, or imprisonment for life, and shall also be liable to fine.
plain_language: Waging, attempting, or helping to wage war against Pakistan carries death or life imprisonment.
legal_scope: Covers treason-equivalent offences: waging war against Pakistan. Death or life imprisonment. Requires government sanction for prosecution (CrPC section 196).
misuse_risks: CrPC section 196 requires prior government sanction. This section has been applied broadly in terrorism-related cases — care needed to distinguish actual treason from political opposition.
use_in_ai_rag: Use for treason and state security offences. Always check prior government sanction requirement under CrPC 196.

title: Sedition
chapter: Chapter VI – Offences Against the State
full_text: Whoever by words, either spoken or written, or by signs, or by visible representation, or otherwise, brings or attempts to bring into hatred or contempt, or excites or attempts to excite disaffection towards, the Federal or Provincial Government established by law shall be punished with imprisonment for life, or imprisonment which may extend to three years, to which fine may be added, or with fine.
plain_language: Sedition — inciting hatred or contempt against the government — carries life imprisonment or up to 3 years, plus possible fine.
legal_scope: Covers sedition against federal or provincial government. Life imprisonment or up to 3 years fine. Requires prior government sanction for cognizance (CrPC 196).
misuse_risks: Highly contested provision. Courts have held that legitimate dissent, criticism, and commentary do not amount to sedition. The law requires active incitement to hatred/contempt — not mere criticism.
use_in_ai_rag: Use with significant caution. Check prior sanction requirement. Distinguish legitimate political criticism (protected) from active incitement to hatred/contempt (may be sedition).

title: Wrongful restraint
chapter: Chapter XVI – Offences Affecting the Human Body
full_text: Whoever voluntarily obstructs any person so as to prevent that person from proceeding in any direction in which that person has a right to proceed, is said wrongfully to restrain that person.
plain_language: Wrongful restraint is preventing a person from moving in a direction they have the right to go.
legal_scope: Defines wrongful restraint. Obstruction must be intentional. Does NOT cover complete confinement (section 340 — wrongful confinement).
misuse_risks: Must be a voluntary/intentional obstruction. Accidental blocking is not wrongful restraint. Distinguish from wrongful confinement (section 340) which is more severe.
use_in_ai_rag: Use for wrongful restraint definition and charges. Check intent and movement restriction. Distinguish from wrongful confinement (340).

title: Wrongful confinement
chapter: Chapter XVI – Offences Affecting the Human Body
full_text: Whoever wrongfully restrains any person in such a manner as to prevent that person from proceeding beyond certain circumscribing limits, is said wrongfully to confine that person.
plain_language: Wrongful confinement is restricting a person within bounds beyond which they cannot move — more complete than mere restraint.
legal_scope: Covers total confinement within limits. More severe than wrongful restraint (section 339). Punishment varies by duration (sections 342-348).
misuse_risks: Duration affects sentencing — extended confinement carries higher penalties. Distinguish from kidnapping (sections 360-363) which involves movement.
use_in_ai_rag: Use for unlawful detention and confinement queries. Apply duration analysis for sentencing (sections 342-348). Distinguish from kidnapping.

title: Attempt to commit culpable homicide
chapter: Chapter XVI – Offences Affecting the Human Body
full_text: Whoever does any act with such intention or knowledge and under such circumstances that, if he by that act caused death, he would be guilty of culpable homicide not amounting to murder, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to fifteen years and shall also be liable to fine; and if hurt is caused to any person by such act, the offender shall be liable either to such punishment as is hereinafter provided or to imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to fifteen years and shall also be liable to fine or both.
plain_language: Attempting to commit culpable homicide not amounting to murder carries up to 15 years imprisonment, plus additional punishment if hurt is actually caused.
legal_scope: Covers attempted culpable homicide (not amounting to murder). Up to 15 years. Distinct from attempted murder (section 324, up to 10 years).
misuse_risks: Note: attempted culpable homicide (307, 15 years) carries higher maximum than attempted murder (324, 10 years) — this counterintuitive result requires careful sentencing analysis.
use_in_ai_rag: Use for non-fatal attack prosecution where death was possible but did not occur. Compare with attempted murder (324) for intent analysis.

title: House-trespass after preparation for hurt, assault or wrongful restraint
chapter: Chapter XVII – Offences Against Property
full_text: Whoever commits house-trespass, having made preparation for causing hurt to any person or for assaulting any person, or for wrongfully restraining any person, or for putting any person in fear of hurt, or of assault, or of wrongful restraint, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine.
plain_language: Breaking into a house while prepared to cause harm, assault, or restrict someone carries up to 7 years imprisonment.
legal_scope: Aggravated house trespass where preparation to harm exists. Requires both house-trespass AND preparation to harm. Does NOT cover ordinary house-trespass (section 448).
misuse_risks: Preparation to harm must be contemporaneous with trespass. Post-entry acquisition of weapons may not satisfy this requirement.
use_in_ai_rag: Use for aggravated house-breaking and trespass with violent intent charges. Check both elements: trespass AND preparation to harm.

title: Search of place suspected to contain stolen property
chapter: Chapter VII – Processes to Compel the Production of Things
full_text: If a District Magistrate, Sub-divisional Magistrate or Magistrate of the first class has reason to believe that any place is used for the deposit or sale of stolen property, or for the deposit, sale, or production of any obscene object referred to in section 292 of the Pakistan Penal Code, he may by warrant authorise any police officer not below the rank of sub-inspector to search such place in the manner specified in the warrant.
plain_language: A Magistrate can authorize police to search a place they believe contains stolen property or obscene materials.
legal_scope: Covers Magistrate-authorized search warrants for stolen property or obscene materials. Does NOT cover search in course of investigation (section 165).
misuse_risks: Search must be authorized — warrantless search of premises (without section 165 emergency justification) is illegal. Material found in illegal search may be inadmissible.
use_in_ai_rag: Use for Magistrate-issued search warrant queries. Distinguish from investigative search without warrant (section 165).

title: Search by police officer
chapter: Chapter XIV – Information to the Police and their Powers of Investigation
full_text: Whenever an officer in charge of a police station or a police officer making an investigation has reasonable grounds for believing that anything necessary to the investigation of an offence is to be found in any place within the limits of the police station of which he is in charge, or to which he is attached, and that such thing cannot be otherwise obtained without undue delay, such officer may, after recording in writing the grounds of his belief and specifying in such writing, as nearly as may be, the thing for which search is to be made, search, or cause search to be made, in any such place for any such thing.
plain_language: Police may search a place without a warrant during investigation if they have reasonable grounds to believe evidence is there and delay would frustrate the search. Grounds must be recorded in writing.
legal_scope: Covers warrantless investigative search by police. Requires reasonable grounds AND recording in writing AND urgency. Does NOT authorize blanket searches.
misuse_risks: Grounds must be recorded BEFORE the search — retroactive justification is not acceptable. Evidence found in illegal search raises admissibility challenges.
use_in_ai_rag: Use for investigative search without warrant queries. Verify three requirements: reasonable grounds, written recording, urgency/undue delay.

title: Persons in charge of closed place to allow ingress
chapter: Chapter VII – Processes to Compel the Production of Things
full_text: Whenever any place liable to search or inspection under this Chapter is closed, any person residing in, or being in charge of, such place, shall, on demand of the officer or other person executing the warrant, and on production of the warrant, allow him free ingress thereto, and afford all reasonable facilities for a search therein.
plain_language: Anyone in charge of a place being lawfully searched must allow police access and provide reasonable search facilities upon presentation of the warrant.
legal_scope: Obliges occupant to allow search on valid warrant. Does NOT require compliance with warrantless searches unless section 165 conditions are met.
misuse_risks: Obligation to allow ingress arises on valid warrant only. Occupant may demand to see warrant before compliance.
use_in_ai_rag: Use for queries about obligation to allow police searches. Key: valid warrant must be produced before compliance is required.

title: Evidence for prosecution in Magistrate trials
chapter: Chapter XX – Trials Before Magistrates
full_text: On the date so fixed, or, if the accused appears or is brought before the Magistrate at an earlier date, as soon as the Magistrate has satisfied himself under section 240 that the accused is ready to face trial, the Magistrate shall proceed to hear the complainant (if any) and take all such evidence as may be produced in support of the prosecution; and shall also, on application of the accused, issue process for compelling the attendance of any witness for the defence.
plain_language: After charge framing, the Magistrate hears the complainant and prosecution evidence. The accused can also apply for defense witnesses to be summoned.
legal_scope: Governs prosecution evidence stage in Magistrate trial. Accused's right to summon defense witnesses must be facilitated. Does NOT address trial completion or judgment.
misuse_risks: Right to summon defense witnesses is fundamental — denial violates Article 10A fair trial rights. Magistrate must actively facilitate, not passively allow, this right.
use_in_ai_rag: Use for Magistrate trial procedure queries on the evidence stage. Emphasize defendant's right to summon and examine defense witnesses.

title: Evidence for defence
chapter: Chapter XX – Trials Before Magistrates
full_text: The accused shall then be called upon to enter upon his defence and to produce his evidence; and if the accused puts in any written statement, the Magistrate shall file it with the record. If the accused, after he has entered upon his defence, applies to the Magistrate to issue any process for compelling the attendance of any witness, the Magistrate shall issue such process unless he considers that such application should be refused on the ground that it is made for the purpose of vexation or delay or for defeating the ends of justice and such ground shall be recorded by him in writing.
plain_language: The accused presents their defense and can summon witnesses. Process for defense witnesses must be issued unless the application is vexatious or for delay.
legal_scope: Covers the defense evidence stage. Right to summon witnesses is a right, refusable only on narrow grounds (vexation/delay/defeating justice). Grounds must be recorded.
misuse_risks: Refusal to issue defense process must be on stated grounds in writing. Blanket refusal violates fair trial rights. Written statement must be filed — it forms part of the record.
use_in_ai_rag: Use for defense evidence stage in Magistrate trial. Key: right to summon witnesses is the rule; refusal must be written and justified.

title: Judgment — how pronounced
chapter: Chapter XXVII – The Judgment
full_text: The judgment in every trial in any Criminal Court of original jurisdiction shall be pronounced in open Court by the presiding officer immediately after the termination of the trial, or at some subsequent time of which notice shall be given to the parties or their pleaders, and it shall be dated and signed by the presiding officer in open Court at the time of pronouncing it.
plain_language: Criminal judgments must be pronounced in open court, signed and dated by the judge at the time of pronouncement. Parties must be notified of the date.
legal_scope: Mandates open court judgment delivery. Signature and date are required at time of pronouncement. Does NOT cover appellate judgment delivery.
misuse_risks: Judgment not pronounced in open court violates procedural requirements. Judgment signed after pronouncement without correction may be challenged.
use_in_ai_rag: Use for queries about criminal judgment procedure and formalities.

title: Language and contents of judgment
chapter: Chapter XXVII – The Judgment
full_text: The judgment shall be written in the language of the Court, and shall contain the point or points for determination, the decision thereon and the reasons for the decision. It shall specify the offence (if any) of which, and the section of the Pakistan Penal Code or other law under which, the accused is convicted, and the punishment to which he is sentenced. If it be a judgment of acquittal, it shall state the offence of which the accused is acquitted and direct that he be set at liberty.
plain_language: A criminal judgment must clearly state the issues, decisions, reasons, the specific offence and legal provision, and the sentence. Acquittal judgments must direct release.
legal_scope: Defines mandatory content of criminal judgments. Both conviction and acquittal requirements are specified. Deficient reasoning can be a ground of appeal.
misuse_risks: Judgments without reasoning violate Article 10A fair trial. Appellate courts can remand for fresh reasoned decision where reasoning is absent or manifestly insufficient.
use_in_ai_rag: Use for judgment quality and content queries. Check for specified points: issues, decision, reasons, offence section, and sentence.

title: Punishment for attempting to commit offences punishable with imprisonment for life or other imprisonment
chapter: Chapter XXIII – Attempts to Commit Offences
full_text: Whoever attempts to commit an offence punishable by this Code with transportation for life or imprisonment, or to cause such an offence to be committed, and in such attempt does any act towards the commission of the offence, shall, where no express provision is made by this Code for the punishment of such attempt, be punished with imprisonment of any description provided for the offence, for a term which may extend to one-half of the imprisonment for life or, as the case may be, one-half of the longest term provided for that offence, or with such fine as is provided for the offence, or with both.
plain_language: Attempting an offence (where no specific attempt provision exists) carries up to half the maximum punishment for the completed offence.
legal_scope: General attempt provision. Only applies where no specific attempt section exists in PPC. Maximum punishment is half the completed offence's maximum.
misuse_risks: Section 511 is the residual provision — specific attempt sections (324, 307, etc.) take precedence. Courts have held that mere preparation is not attempt; an overt act toward commission is required.
use_in_ai_rag: Use as fallback for attempt charges where no specific provision exists. Verify: Is there a specific attempt provision? If yes, use that. If not, apply section 511.
